<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677056 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00554: Global validation rules

- page_id: `169677056`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677056/Global+validation+rules
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-01-11T14:13:37.249+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Validation > Custom validation > Creating new validation rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### EXCERPT: Sample model

INLINE

Global validation rules check the result of an element set, while validation rules contained in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Predefined validation suites' space='MD2024xR1'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Active validation suites' space='MD2024xR1'] are dedicated to check a separate element. You can create global validation rules in the same way as other validation rules by defining an appropriate condition in the**Specification** property.

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating new validation rules' space='MD2024xR1']]]>
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining Constraint condition' space='MD2024xR1']>]]>

For example, the *balanced budget* validation rule (Constraint specification is shown in the following figure) is a global validation rule. It is created for the classifier of the *ReseachProject* Class. The *balanced budget* validation rule condition, defined by using OCL2.0 language in the **Specification** property, expresses that the sum of the expenses of all projects must be less than the sum of all *Sponsor* contributions. This validation rule is global because it includes all instances in the model, rather than only the particular instance of *ResearchProject* that violates the rule.

#### TIP: Sample model

Sample model

An example of a global validation rule can be found in the **model validation** sample model > *Classifiers Validation Rules* [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space='MD2024xR1']. To open this sample do one of the following:

- Download *[ATTACHMENT filename='model validation.mdzip'].*
- Find in the modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\product features\model validation.mdzip.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The specification of the global validation rule named balanced budget.

After the validation finishes, the global validation rule is marked as <model> in the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Validation Results panel' space='MD2024xR1']**Validation Results** panel > **Element** column. This means that the entire model, not the separate element, violates the rule.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The representation of global validation rule in the Validation Results panel.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

You can create global validation rules in the same way as other validation rules by defining an appropriate condition in the<strong> Specification</strong> property.</p><ul><li><span class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Creating new validation rules" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to create validation rules >]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></li><li><span class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Defining Constraint condition" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to define Constraint expression >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></li></ul><p><span class="confluence-link">For example, the <em class="confluence-link">balanced budget</em> validation rule (Constraint specification is shown in the following figure) is a global validation rule. It is created for the classifier of the <em>ReseachProject</em> Class. This means that the entire model, not the separate element, violates the rule.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="global_validation_rule_in_validation_results_panel.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Global validation rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The representation of global validation rule in the Validation Results panel.</h6><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169674422 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00555: Glossary table

- page_id: `169674422`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169674422/Glossary+table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T16:22:53.300+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables
- labels: ['terms']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

219319399

219319402

219328322

**On this page**

219319400

##### **Description**

The Glossary saves time by ensuring consistent usage of terminology in the organization. It improves communication between team members, as terms are understood to have the same definitions and usages everywhere they appear.

Each row in the table represents a term. A word, phrase, or any element of the model can be a term. Words and phrases defined as terms are underlined. This enables you to identify terms in your model.

The word or phrase is underlined only if it completely matches the defined term. For example, if you defined the word *Project* in your glossary, the word *Projects* would not be underlined because it is plural.

##### **Purpose**

The Glossary Table allows you to:

- Create a list of all the specific terms used in the project.
- Define your terms in one place.
- Use them everywhere in your project.
- Export your glossary to a new project and use it in other projects.
- Export your glossary into .html, .csv, or .xlsx files.
- Have several glossaries in one project.
- Customize the representation of the glossary in the table.

##### **Usage**

The video below demonstrates how to use the glossary when working with Requirements.

1000550

##### **Example**

The following figure illustrates the terms defined in the Glossary Table.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The figure below shows the term visibility on the diagram pane. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### **Specific tasks in the Glossary Table**

The following outlines the specific procedures when working with Glossary Tables:

****

219319398

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678606 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00556: Groovy script tool

- page_id: `169678606`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678606/Groovy+script+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-10-28T09:18:15.598+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1357810331

1357810332

1357810332

Groovy Script tool enables report templates to evaluate or run Groovy codes from templates and external Groovy files.

The Groovy tool includes two methods:

**(i) 'eval' method** 
 This method will evaluate Groovy text, and then return the result. 
**(ii) 'execute' method** 
 This method will execute a Groovy file, and then return the result.

Like other Script Tools, the Groovy Script Tool '*scripttool.jar*' must be installed in the 'extensions' folder of the Report Wizard plugin. The same '*groovy-all-1.7.9.jar*' of the Automaton plugin is used, so make sure the Automaton plugin is enabled and loaded. For further information about Custom Tools and the installation, see *[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom tool' space='MD2024xR1']*.

To import Groovy Script tool to a template, type the following code:

```text

```

1357810330

**Related pages**

The same '<em>groovy-all-1.7.9.jar</em>' of the Automaton plugin is used, so make sure the Automaton plugin is enabled and loaded. For further information about Custom Tools and the installation, see <em><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Custom tool" /></ac:link></em>.</p><p>To import Groovy Script tool to a template, type the following code:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d52fdb3f-26d9-4509-9180-f02fbf465806"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#import ('groovy', 'com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.script.GroovyTool')]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7c0f5b2f-4439-4633-99c9-eeb069610521"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1357810330</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="987fab6b-993d-42a2-a0f9-7a7759bd6216" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678607 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00557: Groovy script tool API

- page_id: `169678607`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678607/Groovy+script+tool+API
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-02-10T18:46:14.427+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Groovy script tool
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### 'eval' Method

**eval(String script)**

This method will evaluate a Groovy code from a string and return the result.

```text

```

**eval(String script, String bindingName, Object bindingObject)**

This method will evaluate a Groovy code with a single binding object and specified binding name. The code will be evaluated from a string. The binding name will be used as the name for this object.

```text

```

**eval(String script, Map bindingMap)**

This method will evaluate a Groovy code with a set of binding arguments (a name and an object). The code will be evaluated from a string. The binding map consists of key-value pairs for the binding name and the binding object.

```text

```

Or

```text

```

The second code contains curly brackets; '{' and '}' characters, which are not allowed to be used in any *RTF* template. For the *RTF* template, use the first code instead.

#### 'execute' method

**execute(String filename)**

This method will execute a Groovy file. The 'filename' parameter refers to a name of the Groovy file, an absolute path to the Groovy file, or a relative path starting from the template to the Groovy file.

```text

```

If the Groovy file contains Groovy functions, you can recall the functions by using 'eval()' methods.

```text

```

**execute(String filename, String bindingName, Object bindingObject)**

This method will execute a Groovy file with a single binding object and specified binding name. The 'filename' parameter refers to a name of the Groovy file, an absolute path to the Groovy file, or a relative path starting from the template to the Groovy file. The binding name will be used as the name for this object.

File filename.groovy

```text

```

The template code

```text

```

**execute(String filename, Map bindingMap)**

This method will execute a Groovy file with a set of binding arguments (a name and an object). The 'filename' parameter refers to a name of the Groovy file, an absolute path to the Groovy file, or a relative path starting from the template to the Groovy file. The binding map consists of key-value pairs for the binding name and the binding object.

File filename.groovy

```text

```

The template code

```text

```

- Absolute Path : If the 'filename' is provided with an absolute path, Groovy Tool will read the Groovy file from an absolute location such as $groovy.execute(' c:/mycode/readclass.groovy ') .
- Relative Path : If the 'filename' is provided with a relative path, Groovy Tool will read the template from a relative location. This relative location starts from the current directory in which the template is located such as $groovy.execute(' readclass.groovy ') .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h2>'eval' Method</h2><p><strong>eval(String script)</strong></p><p>This method will evaluate a Groovy code from a string and return the result.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5a33a1f1-f29e-4307-81b9-57862248176f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$groovy.eval("println 'Hello World!'")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>eval(String script, String bindingName, Object bindingObject)</strong></p><p>This method will evaluate a Groovy code with a single binding object and specified binding name. The code will be evaluated from a string. The binding name will be used as the name for this object.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="58fe7cf5-28c9-4058-8c49-b196e383723f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach ($c in $Class)
$groovy.eval("println classname", "classname", $c.name)
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>eval(String script, Map bindingMap)</strong></p><p>This method will evaluate a Groovy code with a set of binding arguments (a name and an object). The code will be evaluated from a string. The binding map consists of key-value pairs for the binding name and the binding object.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c9f6d2fe-35b7-4026-ab7c-eddd9e604484"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ($dict = $map.createHashMap())
#set ($void = $dict.put("first", "foo"))
#set ($void = $dict.put("last", "bar"))
$groovy.eval("println first + last", $dict)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Or</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="78fb6923-74a4-42f2-80ed-7dd51974bb4a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$groovy.eval("println first + ' ' + last", {"first":"foo", "last":"bar"})]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="65cfc62e-bc62-457d-9602-34b93ca8b902"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The second code contains curly brackets; '{' and '}' characters, which are not allowed to be used in any <em>RTF</em> template. For the <em>RTF</em> template, use the first code instead.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2>'execute' method</h2><p><strong>execute(String filename)</strong></p><p>This method will execute a Groovy file. The 'filename' parameter refers to a name of the Groovy file, an absolute path to the Groovy file, or a relative path starting from the template to the Groovy file.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4c45cd41-14fe-49c2-b655-ba8d72fabcca"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$groovy.execute("filename.groovy")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If the Groovy file contains Groovy functions, you can recall the functions by using 'eval()' methods.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="23471ea8-420f-4626-ac33-ceee8da6a8e4"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$groovy.execute('filename.groovy')
$groovy.eval("new groovy().functionname()")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>execute(String filename, String bindingName, Object bindingObject)</strong></p><p>This method will execute a Groovy file with a single binding object and specified binding name. The 'filename' parameter refers to a name of the Groovy file, an absolute path to the Groovy file, or a relative path starting from the template to the Groovy file. The binding name will be used as the name for this object.</p><p>File filename.groovy</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8419f1c5-de5f-440f-ae12-f0bad450ecdc"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA["Class name is " + $c.name]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The template code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1977f794-8fcd-4669-8b9c-2dcab9cdb9be"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach ($c in $Class)
$groovy.execute("filename.groovy", 'c', $c)
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>execute(String filename, Map bindingMap)</strong></p><p>This method will execute a Groovy file with a set of binding arguments (a name and an object). The 'filename' parameter refers to a name of the Groovy file, an absolute path to the Groovy file, or a relative path starting from the template to the Groovy file. The binding map consists of key-value pairs for the binding name and the binding object.</p><p>File filename.groovy</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="be28b4ed-e9b6-4192-a2e9-286c4e728837"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[first + " " + last]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The template code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cfecb802-5ca5-4f91-a3e4-78cec3eeb108"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ($dict = $map.createHashMap())
#set ($void = $dict.put("first", "foo"))
#set ($void = $dict.put("last", "bar"))
$groovy.execute("filename.groovy", $dict)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7e460a72-333c-46ee-b0de-6d1efa48f9c6"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><em>Absolute Path</em>: If the 'filename' is provided with an absolute path, Groovy Tool will read the Groovy file from an absolute location such as <strong>$groovy.execute('</strong><em>c:/mycode/readclass.groovy</em><strong>')</strong>.</li><li><em>Relative Path</em>: If the 'filename' is provided with a relative path, Groovy Tool will read the template from a relative location. This relative location starts from the current directory in which the template is located such as <strong>$groovy.execute('</strong><em>readclass.groovy</em><strong>')</strong>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1267488958

1267488960

2050330875

**On this page**

43

1267488960

###### **$group.create()**

Create a new instance of a group tool.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | - | - | - |
| Return | - | GroupTool | An instance of a group tool. |

****

###### **$group.init()**

Initialize a group tool.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | - | - | - |
| Return | - | void | - |

****

###### **$group.groupNames()**

Return a set of group names.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | - | - | - |
| Return | - | java.util.Set | A set of group names. |

****

###### **$group.contains(groupName)**

Test whether a group name is contained in a set of group names.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | groupName | java.lang.String | A group name. |
| Return | - | - | True if the group name is contained in a set of group names, otherwise false. |

****

###### **$group.put(groupName, object)**

Add an object into a group.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | groupName | java.lang.String | A group name. |
|  | object | java.lang.Object | An object being added. |
| Return | - | void | - |

****

###### **$group.get(groupName)**

Return a list of group objects.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | groupName | java.lang.String | A group name. |
| Return | - | java.util.List | A list of group objects. |

###### ****

###### **$group.remove(groupName)**

Remove a group from the specified group name.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | groupName | java.lang.String | A group name. |
| Return | - | java.util.List | A list of group objects previously associated with a specified group name, or null if there is no group corresponding to the key. |

****

###### **$group.removeAll()**

Remove all groups.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | - | - | - |
| Return | - | void |  |

****

###### **$group.clear()**

Remove all mappings.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | - | - | - |
| Return | - | void | - |

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679023 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00559: Grouping Tags inside Stereotype

- page_id: `169679023`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679023/Grouping+Tags+inside+Stereotype
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-08-16T11:49:47.528+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Working with Profiles > Creating Profiles
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When a stereotype has many properties, it is very useful to group these properties into named groups. For this purpose, every property could have a tagged value with its group name.

To group tags

1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'] of the stereotype property.
2. Click the **Tags** property group.
3. Select the **groupName** property and assign a value for it:
  1. Click the **Edit Value** button
  2. In the **Value** area, type a group name. . [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_property_group.png']
4. Repeat these steps for every stereotype property.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying Stereotypes' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Tag' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p><span>When a stereotype has many properties, it is very useful to group these properties into named groups. For this purpose, every property could have a tagged value with its group name. </span></p><p><span>To group tags</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span>Open the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link> of the stereotype property.</span></li><li><span>Click the <strong>Tags</strong> property group.</span></li><li><span>Select the <strong>groupName</strong> property and assign a value for it:</span><ol><li><span>Click the <strong>Edit Value</strong> button</span></li><li><span>In the <strong>Value </strong> area, type a group name.</span><br /><br />.<ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="creating_property_group.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Grouping Tags inside Stereotype" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol></li><li>Repeat these steps for every stereotype property. </li></ol></div></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b14401e6-4bad-4948-8ab5-88b81d7034fe"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related Pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Applying Stereotypes" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Stereotype" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Tag" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

## PAGE 00560: Guard on decision

- page_id: `169676935`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676935/Guard+on+decision
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2022-11-15T11:37:51.320+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Validation > UML validation rules > UML completeness constraints rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

DecisionGuar

**Description**

This validation rule checks if a Flow from a Decision Node has a Guard specified.

**Severity**

warning

**Constrained Element**

ActivityEdge

**Solvers**

- To fix this, make sure to specify a Guard for a Flow from a Decision Node.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Decision Node has two outgoing Object Flows, one of which has no Guard. The error is resolved by specifying the Guard for the second Flow.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679659 space=MD2024xR1 version=3 -->
## PAGE 00561: Guidelines for Working with Large Models

- page_id: `169679659`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679659/Guidelines+for+Working+with+Large+Models
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-08-05T12:18:54.505+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: UsageInDiagrams is Deprecated

2033054156

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: UsageInDiagrams is Deprecated

2033054168

2033054154

**On this page**

**42**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: UsageInDiagrams is Deprecated

2033054158

Product performance can be highly dependent on the model organization strategy. From long-term work experience with various clients, we have selected the biggest challenges and gathered their possible solutions in one place.

##### Teamwork Cloud Operations SpecificsTeamwork Cloud Operations Specifics

###### **Project Opening**

Opening Teamwork Cloud project for the first time may take a bit longer, as all project-related data has to be downloaded from the server and then processed in the modeling client. The download time depends on the project size and network speed, e.g., for large projects (containing 5M/3.5M model elements in "19.0 and before/2021x and later" version), it may require downloading 1GB or more of data. Once the project is opened, we store your project data locally. The processing time depends on the downloaded project size. Opening the project for the second time is much faster as there is no need to request all data from the server again. Instead, the previously downloaded and locally stored data is reused. Only new information has to be downloaded from the server. The maximum processed data count is specified by the **Recent Files List Size** environment option. The processed project data is always preserved when the project is saved offline. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opening projects from Teamwork Cloud' space='']. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Open Server Project dialog.

###### Committing

Frequent commits make it easier for other team members to track project changes; however, this should be avoided if many users are working on a particular project. Frequent commits create a lot of project versions and increase the commit time due to the need to update the project every time. You need to decide upon the best-committing strategy within your company. A widely used practice is to commit the changes at the end of the work day; however, this should be avoided because, in some cases, the project commit may take longer than expected. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud' space=''].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Switching Used Project versions

Teamwork Cloud projects are usually built from smaller parts. Different teams simultaneously develop different parts of the project, so there is a need to keep the model up-to-date. A user is notified when a new version of the used project arrives, and it is up to the user whether to apply the update or not. As not all used project version updates are important, there is no need to update the version each time a new version arrives. Therefore, it is best to set up a version update strategy within the teams. You can decide whether or not to switch to a new used project version by considering the severity of that version – the commit tags can be used to mark that the particular version contains the major changes and should be used instead. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Changing the used project version' space=''].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Notification about version changes.

In some cases, the project version update may take longer than expected due to a local cache update.

**Used Projects Auto Update Plugin**. This plugin allows users to set up specific used projects to update. We recommend that you schedule it to launch at night so that the latest versions will be ready to use the next work day. More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Used Projects Auto Update Plugin' space=''].

###### Merge

To perform a merge operation, the ancestor and contributor (source and target) projects have to be loaded, meaning that the memory consumption increases at least two times compared to the memory required to perform daily tasks in the project. After testing the memory consumption with different-sized projects, the following minimum memory requirement for the merge operation was identified: *2 x (memory required to load project) + 1 GB** .*These memory requirements are indicative and may vary by project. It strongly depends on the number of detected changes between merged versions (in this case, the project merge was done with 1.5k changes in total).

Since the merge operation uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. If the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. In case of excessive memory usage, the merge operation can be optimized for lower memory consumption by excluding differing diagrams/elements count (**Optimize for** > **Memory** in the **Merge Projects** dialog); however, this optimization slows down the merge operation. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model merge in Teamwork Cloud' space=''].

###### Project Decomposition

Model partitioning enables a more granular model management. Despite the benefits of granularity, project decomposition should be done carefully since managing used projects can become a bottleneck from a performance perspective. Please follow the criteria below to decompose a project:

- 20+ active users work on the same project simultaneously. As a result, the project contains many project versions, which slow down project history-related operations. Also, a higher number of users leads to a higher number of commits, resulting in longer update times and slowing down the commit operation.
- A large number of models owned by the same person indicates that the model granularity is too small or there is a need to distribute responsibilities.
- The part of the project (such as type libraries) should be used in other projects.
- Different teams work on a certain aspect of the system separately. All these separate parts are then connected in the integration project.

###### Publishing projects to Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud

As publishing greatly depends on the element count and diagram complexity (e.g., diagram type, the number of elements displayed, dynamic or static content, etc.), please consider the publishing scope before publishing. Since publishing uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. Remember that if the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing Cameo Collaborator documents' space='CC4TWC2024xR1'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Publishing a model is the first step in the workflow for reviewing models and providing feedback.

###### Content History

If retrieving a particular element history takes a lot of time, it implies that the Content History action should be performed carefully as it retrieves the history of all contained elements at once. Before invoking this action, you should carefully consider the scope of the elements contained by the element for which the action is invoked. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reviewing content history' space=''].[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### History of element changes in the scope of a *Book*package.

##### Teamwork Cloud Operations Troubleshooting

**Waiting for Response**

If the operation that requires a remote call to Teamwork Cloud is taking too long, you can analyze a *.log* file to learn how much time was spent processing the operation on the client and server-side.

Where do I find a .*log* file?

1. Open your modeling tool.
2. In the main menu, go to Help > About <modeling tool name> .
3. In the Environment tab, click the file path next to Log File to open a *.log* file.

**Measuring Network Latency**

*INFO NetworkPerformance - <operation name> latency <latency time>*

**Description**

The latency (i.e., the time it takes for the ping signal sent from the tool to the server to return) is measured for the following operations:

- Login
- Committing project to server
- Updating project
- Opening project

Example: INFO NetworkPerformance - Login latency 1 ms

**Remote Operations Duration Statistics**

*INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - <operation name> all operations time <total operation time>*

*INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - <operation name> calls total time: <total time, spent in remote calls>, start count: <remote calls count>, failed count: <failed remote calls count>*

**Description**

The remote and local operations duration statistics are collected for the following operations:

- Updating from local project
- Opening Manage Projects dialog
- Opening Open Server Project dialog
- Updating project
- Committing changes to a server project
- Setting selected version as latest
- Cloning project
- Managing version properties
- Opening Teamwork Cloud project
- Renaming project
- Removing project
- Adding project to server
- Viewing element history
- Comparing projects
- Using server project
- Discarding changes
- Locking and unlocking elements/diagrams
- Changing used project version

Example:

- INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects all operations time 9797 ms
- INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects calls total time: 2293 ms, start count: 47, failed count: 0

##### Common Features

###### Validation

The validation engine helps to keep the model accurate, complete, and correct. Since model checking is a complicated task that greatly depends on the project size, please consider the following recommendations to keep the modeling tool running smoothly:

- Set the Validation Scope to narrow down the list of validated elements.
- Set the Validate Only Visible Diagrams project option to true to limit a validation scope to visible diagrams only. Since 2021x version, all projects are migrated to validate only visible diagrams. This is a key option to keep the modeling tool running smoothly while working on large models.
- Set the Exclude Elements from Used Read-Only Project project option to true to skip the validation of elements used projects contain. [ATTACHMENT filename='validation_project_options.png']

###### Querying the Model

Ineffectively written queries can cause showstopper problems, especially when a project becomes quite large. The most attention should be paid to Script, Find, and Find Usage in Diagrams operations. Be aware that the slow execution of queries can noticeably impact the modeling experience - some operations trigger an expression (e.g., smart packages and derived properties) recalculation.

- The Find operation should not be performed for the whole project scope when specifying expressions for smart packages, derived properties, validation rules, etc. Since the operation is highly dependent on the search scope - the wider it is, the longer it takes - it can noticeably impact the modeling experience by slowing down the common modeling tool commands that, as a result, cause the expression recalculation.
- It is not a good practice to use Find for searching common elements (e.g., Class, Package, Block, etc.).
- Searching element usages in diagrams should be used carefully since the operation searches for element usages in dynamic content. UsageInDiagrams is DeprecatedThis operation is very expensive. It not only checks symbolic diagrams for elements but also loads and builds Generic Tables, Dependency Matrices, Relationship Maps, etc. It should be best avoided and used only if you understand the possible consequence of building all diagrams in the project.Instead of it, please use the **UsageInDiagramsWithSymbols**operation****that searches for usages only in symbolic diagrams. Other diagrams, such as Generic Tables, Dependency Matrices, Relationship Maps, etc., are not searched. This operation performs fast and does not load any diagrams to get the information.
- Smart Package specifics:
  - How does this affect performance? The Smart Package content is calculated on demand and cached. Next time on content request, the cached value is used. The cache is cleared on any model change. So the impact on performance depends on the performance of the content calculation, content request frequency, and model change frequency. Examples :
    - Model Browser: if a Smart Package is visible in the Model Browser, it will be refreshed each time after the cache is invalidated (model change occurs, e.g., a new element is created or an existing element is removed). So if a Smart Package is added directly under the root, it will always be visible in the Containment Tree (after opening a project, the root is expanded by default).
    - Validation: if the Smart Package is within the validation scope and the "Exclude elements from additional content" option is disabled, running validation will recalculate Smart Packages if the cache is invalidated.
    - Suspect links: Show Suspicions action triggers the calculation of Smart Packages that fall within the Tracking Scope. Starting with 2021x, the calculation of Smart Packages is no longer triggered.
    - Tables/matrices: if the Smart Package is within the table/matrix scope, it will be calculated by default. Starting with 2021x Refresh1, the content of Smart Packages is not included by default - the Include Additional Content table/matrix option was introduced for that.
  - The Use In Selection Dialogs option - set to false if a slow-running query is defined. [ATTACHMENT filename='Use_In_Selection_Dialogs_option.png']
  - Avoid nesting Smart Packages, i.e., using one Smart Package as part of another Smart Package's content.
  - Freezing the contents of a Smart Package . If you do not want the contents of the Smart Package to be updated automatically, you can suspend the calculation of the Smart Package contents. As a result, elements gathered according to a set of criteria specified in the Query dialog are no longer automatically added to the Smart Package contents. You can undo this at any time by changing the Frozen state.

The chart below shows how the Legend item condition execution time differs by performing the same expression in different languages. We advise choosing the most efficiently performing languages which are indicated as recommended (StructuredExpression, Groovy).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A chart comparing the Legend item condition execution time by language.

###### **Troubleshooting**

You can use the Evaluate Operation Performance capability to see the performance diagnostics that help determine the reasons behind slow tool operations if they are caused by the execution of expressions (e.g., derived properties, smart packages, legends, validation rules) defined in the model. Matrix and table building can also be analyzed.

For example, you noticed that opening a particular diagram took longer than expected. A progress bar titled "Adorning" popped up for a few seconds before the diagram opened. To know what caused this performance issue, run the Evaluate Operation Performance tool. In its dialog, click the 'Start' button, thenopen the diagram again reproducing the slow operation, and onceit is done, click the 'Stop' button to see the results. You can use this information to investigate the particular expression and improve its efficiency.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluating operation performance' space=''].

###### **Diagrams**

The consumed memory increases while working with the project for some time. One of the reasons is the number of open diagrams. As high memory usage becomes a problem, we recommend that you do not keep unused diagrams open. To prevent keeping a large number of open diagrams, beginning with version 2021x, the open diagrams limit was introduced with 20 diagrams set as the default value.

Please find below how memory consumption increases depending on the size of a diagram/table:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Chart depicting memory consumption by diagram size (symbols/rows displayed in a diagram/table).

- The matrix/table building time greatly depends on the specified scope - the wider it is, the more time it takes to build the matrix/table.
- Please avoid setting the entire model as the scope even if the matrix/table building performs well, as it can cause unacceptable performance in the following circumstances:
  - a project containing such a table/matrix is used in another project;
  - the model evolves over time, so the table will eventually reach unscalable sizes.
- Also, it is not advisable to choose more than 10 element types to be displayed because this may lead to a decrease in the matrix/table building time. Since matrices/tables have an option to define a scope query, the Smart Packages should not be set as scopes as well.

###### Working with Large Tables

The process of calculating all data in large tables (containing thousands of rows) can be slow and inefficient. For the most efficient performance, we suggest the following:

- Avoid creating tables that exceed ±50 000 cells as that can affect the performance of actions performed on the table data; in such a case, it is recommended to create multiple tables instead.
- Switching to partial loading. Data is loaded only in the visible area, so the most common tasks are performed quickly. More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Partial loading in tables' space=''] . [ATTACHMENT filename='partial_loading_options.png']

###### Working with Model Browser

The organization of the model is determined by the package hierarchy you create to partition the elements in the model into logically cohesive groups. This affects the model browser performance as well - to avoid sluggish browsing in Containment Tree, you need to organize your model to avoid 10k and more directly owned elements by the particular element. Since the containment model browser is a lazy tree, it calculates and represents the nodes while expanding the owner. A large number of owned elements can cause unexpected waiting time to complete the task. So, we recommend splitting elements by creating different packages.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A model structured into packages.

##### Virtual Environments

- Two issues have been identified in VMWare ESX environments. To help prevent problems on the Teamwork Cloud side, we have come up with recommended hardware configurations.

1. 
  1. Overcommitting of resources . The same host has an over-allocation of CPU/RAM. The bigger problem is the over-allocation of RAM. For example, let's say a machine has 1TB of physical RAM. You allocate more than 1TB across various VM's in the host. What happens is that the RAM is no longer physical RAM, but rather mapped to disk, so performance decreases. Xen (Citrix) and KVM do not allow you to over-commit. In these, you can allocate min/max but never exceed the physical RAM, and as more instances start using up the RAM, the system reduces the allocated memory.
  2. Thin disk provisioning . This arises from an over-allocation of disk resources. Let's say you have a given amount of disk space to be used among VM's. In many cases, the admin does not know how much the actual disk usage is required per machine. Some VM's may use more, some less. Therefore, you over-allocate the storage using thin provisioning. Under thin provisioning, only the amount of storage being used is allocated to the virtual disk. As utilization increases, the virtual disk (which is a file) grows. This, however, has a negative impact on the performance of input/output-intensive applications like databases. For Cassandra, it is a killer in performance because compaction can potentially temporarily double the on-disk size. All disks must be thick provisioned.

- The other item is that, at the very least, you need to be using SAS-SSD. If using SAS-SSD, the host needs to have high-end caching controllers. On the Teamwork Cloud side, the core count is important - the more cores, the better.
- SAN's are not recommended. Only NVMe (all flash storage) will provide the necessary performance for large installations, provided that they are properly configured and that enough bandwidth is allocated to the instances.
- It is recommended that you stay away from Windows deployments. There is no freely available Cassandra Windows installer.
- If we look at cloud hosting, the older instances in Amazon use the Xen hypervisor (xcp), whereas the new ones use the kvm hypervisor. These are the instances you should use: preferably, the NVMe-based i3 instances, next would be r5, and finally m5. What is critical is to provision the filesystem properly.
- On the client side, the same issues of resources over-allocation can be a performance killer. Thin provisioning affects performance because disk caches are used extensively. Therefore, what is more, important when using VDI's is working on machines that have faster processors. When provisioning the VDI's, we do not recommend that you allocate a lot of CPU's because they will not be used. It is important to have the fastest processors.

##### Antivirus

Since some antivirus software can cause a significant performance decrease upon opening a project and performing other actions, we recommend that you exclude the modeling tool's home directory from the antivirus scanning scope.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="81e95404-cab0-41c7-b545-2047469b80a6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2033054156</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="47231aad-b8da-4fe1-90e5-190a99247b87"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2033054168</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f8a2bb67-6f28-4baf-86e0-c8ef7704238a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2033054154</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="89bd7402-235e-4b97-9cb0-5a6430439222"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="57a56a15-50a7-44e8-bb1b-f0ad0397ed89"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2033054158</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Product performance can be highly dependent on the model organization strategy. From long-term work experience with various clients, we have selected the biggest challenges and gathered their possible solutions in one place.</p><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5e2b8224-4af5-41e7-bad9-16cf4775b4fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="">Teamwork Cloud Operations Specifics</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Teamwork Cloud Operations Specifics</h3><h4><strong>Project Opening</strong></h4><p>Opening Teamwork Cloud project for the first time may take a bit longer, as all project-related data has to be downloaded from the server and then processed in the modeling client. Different teams simultaneously develop different parts of the project, so there is a need to keep the model up-to-date. A user is notified when a new version of the used project arrives, and it is up to the user whether to apply the update or not. As not all used project version updates are important, there is no need to update the version each time a new version arrives. Therefore, it is best to set up a version update strategy within the teams. You can decide whether or not to switch to a new used project version by considering the severity of that version – the commit tags can be used to mark that the particular version contains the major changes and should be used instead. <br />More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Changing the used project version" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Update_used_projects.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Notification about version changes.</h6><p>In some cases, the project version update may take longer than expected due to a local cache update.</p><p><strong>Used Projects Auto Update Plugin</strong>. This plugin allows users to set up specific used projects to update. We recommend that you schedule it to launch at night so that the latest versions will be ready to use the next work day. More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Used Projects Auto Update Plugin" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h4>Merge</h4><p>To perform a merge operation, the ancestor and contributor (source and target) projects have to be loaded, meaning that the memory consumption increases at least two times compared to the memory required to perform daily tasks in the project. After testing the memory consumption with different-sized projects, the following minimum memory requirement for the merge operation was identified: <em> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">2 x (memory required to load project) + 1 GB*</span> </em> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.<br /></span>*These memory requirements are indicative and may vary by project. It strongly depends on the number of detected changes between merged versions (in this case, the project merge was done with 1.5k changes in total).</p><p>Since the merge operation uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. If the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. In case of excessive memory usage, the merge operation can be optimized for lower memory consumption by excluding differing diagrams/elements count (<strong>Optimize for</strong> &gt; <strong>Memory</strong> in the <strong>Merge Projects</strong> dialog); however, this optimization slows down the merge operation.<br />More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model merge in Teamwork Cloud" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h4><span style="font-weight: 600;letter-spacing: -0.003em;">Project Decomposition</span></h4><p>Model partitioning enables a more granular model management. Despite the benefits of granularity, project decomposition should be done carefully since managing used projects can become a bottleneck from a performance perspective. Please follow the criteria below to decompose a project:</p><ul><li><p>20+ active users work on the same project simultaneously. As a result, the project contains many project versions, which slow down project history-related operations. Also, a higher number of users leads to a higher number of commits, resulting in longer update times and slowing down the commit operation.</p></li><li><p>A large number of models owned by the same person indicates that the model granularity is too small or there is a need to distribute responsibilities.</p></li><li>The part of the project (such as type libraries) should be used in other projects.</li><li>Different teams work on a certain aspect of the system separately. All these separate parts are then connected in the integration project.</li></ul><h4>Publishing projects to Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud</h4><p>As publishing greatly depends on the element count and diagram complexity (e.g., diagram type, the number of elements displayed, dynamic or static content, etc.), please consider the publishing scope before publishing. Since publishing uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. Remember that if the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. <br />More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CC4TWC2024xR1" ri:content-title="Publishing Cameo Collaborator documents" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="main_workflow_publishing.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Publishing a model is the first step in the workflow for reviewing models and providing feedback.</h6><h4>Content History</h4><p style="margin-left: 0.0in;text-align: justify;">If retrieving a particular element history takes a lot of time, it implies that the Content History action should be performed carefully as it retrieves the history of all contained elements at once. Before invoking this action, you should carefully consider the scope of the elements contained by the element for which the action is invoked. <br />More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Reviewing content history" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.<ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Content_history.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">History of element changes in the scope of a <em>Book </em>package.</h6><h3>Teamwork Cloud Operations Troubleshooting</h3><p><strong>Waiting for Response</strong></p><p>If the operation that requires a remote call to Teamwork Cloud is taking too long, you can analyze a <em>.log</em> file to learn how much time was spent processing the operation on the client and server-side.</p><p class="title">Where do I find a .<em>log</em> file?</p><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Open your modeling tool.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the main menu, go to </span><strong>Help</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> &gt;</span> <strong>About &lt;modeling tool name&gt;</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the </span><strong>Environment </strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">tab, click the file path next to</span> <strong>Log File </strong> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">to open a <em>.log</em> file.</span></li></ol><p><strong>Measuring Network Latency</strong></p><p><em>INFO  NetworkPerformance - &lt;operation name&gt; latency &lt;latency time&gt; </em></p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The latency (i.e., the time it takes for the ping signal sent from the tool to the server to return) is measured for the following operations:</p><ul><li>Login</li><li>Committing project to server</li><li>Updating project</li><li>Opening project<br /><br /></li></ul><p class="title">Example: INFO  NetworkPerformance - Login latency 1 ms</p><p><strong>Remote Operations Duration Statistics</strong></p><p><em>INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - &lt;operation name&gt; all operations time &lt;total operation time&gt;</em></p><p><em>INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - &lt;operation name&gt; calls total time: &lt;total time, spent in remote calls&gt;, start count: &lt;remote calls count&gt;, failed count: &lt;failed remote calls count&gt;</em></p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The remote and local operations duration statistics are collected for the following operations:</p><ul><li>Updating from local project</li><li>Opening<strong> Manage Projects</strong> dialog</li><li>Opening <strong>Open Server Project</strong> dialog</li><li>Updating project</li><li>Committing changes to a server project</li><li>Setting selected version as latest</li><li>Cloning project</li><li>Managing version properties</li><li>Opening Teamwork Cloud project</li><li>Renaming project</li><li>Removing project</li><li>Adding project to server</li><li>Viewing element history</li><li>Comparing projects</li><li>Using server project</li><li>Discarding changes</li><li>Locking and unlocking elements/diagrams</li><li>Changing used project version</li></ul><p class="title">Example: </p><ul><li>INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects all operations time 9797 ms</li><li>INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects calls total time: 2293 ms, start count: 47, failed count: 0</li></ul><h3>Common Features</h3><h4>Validation</h4><p>The validation engine helps to keep the model accurate, complete, and correct. Since model checking is a complicated task that greatly depends on the project size, please consider the following recommendations to keep the modeling tool running smoothly:</p><ul><li>Set the <strong>Validation Scope</strong><em> </em>to narrow down the list of validated elements.</li><li>Set the <strong>Validate Only Visible Diagrams</strong><em> </em>project option to <em>true </em>to limit a validation scope to visible diagrams only. Since 2021x version, all projects are migrated to validate only visible diagrams. This is a key option to keep the modeling tool running smoothly while working on large models.</li><li>Set the <strong>Exclude Elements from Used Read-Only Project </strong>project option to <em>true </em>to skip the validation of elements used projects contain.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="validation_project_options.png" /></ac:image></li></ul><h4>Querying the Model</h4><p style="margin-left: 0.0cm;text-align: justify;">Ineffectively written queries can cause showstopper problems, especially when a project becomes quite large. The most attention should be paid to Script, Find, and Find Usage in Diagrams operations. Be aware that the slow execution of queries can noticeably impact the modeling experience - some operations trigger an expression (e.g., smart packages and derived properties) recalculation. </p><ul><li>The Find operation should not be performed for the whole project scope when specifying expressions for smart packages, derived properties, validation rules, etc. Since the operation is highly dependent on the search scope - the wider it is, the longer it takes - it can noticeably impact the modeling experience by slowing down the common modeling tool commands that, as a result, cause the expression recalculation.</li><li><p>It is not a good practice to use Find for searching common elements (e.g., Class, Package, Block, etc.).</p></li><li><p>Searching element usages in diagrams should be used carefully since the operation searches for element usages in dynamic content.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3d7dd41f-de74-4add-be1c-69c023c0427f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">UsageInDiagrams is Deprecated</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p style="text-align: left;">This operation is very expensive. It not only checks symbolic diagrams for elements but also loads and builds Generic Tables, Dependency Matrices, Relationship Maps, etc. It should be best avoided and used only if you understand the possible consequence of building all diagrams in the project.</p><p style="text-align: left;">Instead of it, please use the <strong style="text-align: left;">UsageInDiagramsWithSymbols </strong>operation<strong style="text-align: left;"> </strong>that s<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">earches for usages only in symbolic diagrams. Other diagrams, such as Generic Tables, Dependency Matrices, Relationship Maps, etc., are not searched. This operation performs fast and does not load any diagrams to get the information.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Smart Package specifics:<br /><ul><li><strong>How does this affect performance? </strong>The Smart Package content is calculated on demand and cached. Next time on content request, the cached value is used. The cache is cleared on any model change. So the impact on performance depends on the performance of the content calculation, content request frequency, and model change frequency.<br /><strong>Examples</strong>:<ul><li>Model Browser: if a Smart Package is visible in the Model Browser, it will be refreshed each time after the cache is invalidated (model change occurs, e.g., a new element is created or an existing element is removed). So if a Smart Package is added directly under the root, it will always be visible in the Containment Tree (after opening a project, the root is expanded by default).</li><li>Validation: if the Smart Package is within the validation scope and the &quot;Exclude elements from additional content&quot; option is disabled, running validation will recalculate Smart Packages if the cache is invalidated.</li><li>Suspect links: <strong>Show Suspicions</strong> action triggers the calculation of Smart Packages that fall within the Tracking Scope. Starting with 2021x, the calculation of Smart Packages is no longer triggered.</li><li>Tables/matrices: if the Smart Package is within the table/matrix scope, it will be calculated by default. Starting with 2021x Refresh1, the content of Smart Packages is not included by default - the <strong>Include Additional Content</strong> table/matrix option was introduced for that.</li></ul></li><li>The <strong>Use In Selection Dialogs</strong> option - set to <em>false</em> if a slow-running query is defined.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Use_In_Selection_Dialogs_option.png" /></ac:image></li><li>Avoid nesting Smart Packages, i.e., using one Smart Package as part of another Smart Package's content.</li><li><strong>Freezing the contents of a Smart Package</strong>. If you do not want the contents of the Smart Package to be updated automatically, you can suspend the calculation of the Smart Package contents. As a result, elements gathered according to a set of criteria specified in the <strong>Query</strong> dialog are no longer automatically added to the Smart Package contents. You can undo this at any time by changing the <strong>Frozen</strong> state. <br /><ac:image ac:alt="frozen_enabled.png"><ri:url ri:value="https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/70410160/frozen_enabled.png?version=1&amp;modificationDate=1616502291005&amp;api=v2" /></ac:image></li></ul></li></ul><p>The chart below shows how the Legend item condition execution time differs by performing the same expression in different languages. We advise choosing the most efficiently performing languages which are indicated as recommended (StructuredExpression, Groovy).</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Scripting_languages_performance.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-gray-bolder,#626f86);">A chart comparing the Legend item condition execution time by language.</span></h6><h4><strong>Troubleshooting</strong></h4><p>You can use the Evaluate Operation Performance capability to see the performance diagnostics that help determine the reasons behind slow tool operations if they are caused by the execution of expressions (e.g., derived properties, smart packages, legends, validation rules) defined in the model. Matrix and table building can also be analyzed.</p><p><span>For example, you noticed that opening a particular diagram took longer than expected. A progress bar titled &quot;Adorning&quot; popped up for a few seconds before the diagram opened. To know what caused this performance issue, run the Evaluate Operation Performance tool. In its dialog, click </span> <span>the 'Start' button, then </span><span>open the diagram again reproducing the slow operation, and once </span><span>it is done, click the 'Stop' button to see the results. </span> <span>You can use this information to investigate the particular expression and improve its efficiency.</span></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Evaluating_operation_performance.png" /></ac:image></p><p class="auto-cursor-target">More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Evaluating operation performance" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h4><strong>Diagrams</strong></h4><p style="margin-left: 0.0cm;">The consumed memory increases while working with the project for some time. One of the reasons is the number of open diagrams. As high memory usage becomes a problem, we recommend that you do not keep unused diagrams open. To prevent keeping a large number of open diagrams, beginning with version 2021x, the open diagrams limit was introduced with 20 diagrams set as the default value.</p><p style="margin-left: 0.0cm;">Please find below how memory consumption increases depending on the size of a diagram/table:</p><p style="margin-left: 0.0cm;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="memory_consumption_of_tables_and_diagrams_with_symbols.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="margin-left: 0.0cm;text-align: center;">Chart depicting memory consumption by diagram size (symbols/rows displayed in a diagram/table).</h6><ul><li>The matrix/table building time greatly depends on the specified scope - the wider it is, the more time it takes to build the matrix/table. </li><li style="list-style-type: none;"><p>Please avoid setting the entire model as the scope even if the matrix/table building performs well, as it can cause unacceptable performance in the following circumstances:</p><ul><li>a project containing such a table/matrix is used in another project;</li><li>the model evolves over time, so the table will eventually reach unscalable sizes.</li></ul></li><li>Also, it is not advisable to choose more than 10 element types to be displayed because this may lead to a decrease in the matrix/table building time. Since matrices/tables have an option to define a scope query, the Smart Packages should not be set as scopes as well.</li></ul><h4>Working with Large Tables</h4><p>The process of calculating all data in large tables (containing thousands of rows) can be slow and inefficient. For the most efficient performance, we suggest the following:</p><ul><li>Avoid creating tables that exceed ±50 000 cells as that can affect the performance of actions performed on the table data; in such a case, it is recommended to create multiple tables instead.</li><li>Switching to partial loading. Data is loaded only in the visible area, so the most common tasks are performed quickly. More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Partial loading in tables" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="partial_loading_options.png" /></ac:image></li></ul><h4>Working with Model Browser</h4><p class="p1">The organization of the model is determined by the package hierarchy you create to partition the elements in the model into logically cohesive groups. This affects the model browser performance as well - to avoid sluggish browsing in Containment Tree, you need to organize your model to avoid 10k and more directly owned elements by the particular element. Since the containment model browser is a lazy tree, it calculates and represents the nodes while expanding the owner. A large number of owned elements can cause unexpected waiting time to complete the task. So, we recommend splitting elements by creating different packages.</p><p class="p1"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="model_browser_structure.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 0.0cm;">A model structured into packages.</h6><h3>Virtual Environments</h3><ul><li>Two issues have been identified in VMWare ESX environments. To help prevent problems on the Teamwork Cloud side, we have come up with recommended hardware configurations.</li></ul><ol><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ol><li><strong>Overcommitting of resources</strong>. The same host has an over-allocation of CPU/RAM. The bigger problem is the over-allocation of RAM. For example, let's say a machine has 1TB of physical RAM. You allocate more than 1TB across various VM's in the host. What happens is that the RAM is no longer physical RAM, but rather mapped to disk, so performance decreases. Xen (Citrix) and KVM do not allow you to over-commit. In these, you can allocate min/max but never exceed the physical RAM, and as more instances start using up the RAM, the system reduces the allocated memory.</li><li><strong>Thin disk provisioning</strong>. This arises from an over-allocation of disk resources. Let's say you have a given amount of disk space to be used among VM's. In many cases, the admin does not know how much the actual disk usage is required per machine. Some VM's may use more, some less. Therefore, you over-allocate the storage using thin provisioning. Under thin provisioning, only the amount of storage being used is allocated to the virtual disk. As utilization increases, the virtual disk (which is a file) grows. This, however, has a negative impact on the performance of input/output-intensive applications like databases. For Cassandra, it is a killer in performance because compaction can potentially temporarily double the on-disk size. All disks must be thick provisioned.</li></ol></li></ol><ul><li>The other item is that, at the very least, you need to be using SAS-SSD. If using SAS-SSD, the host needs to have high-end caching controllers. On the Teamwork Cloud side, the core count is important - the more cores, the better.</li><li>SAN's are not recommended. Only NVMe (all flash storage) will provide the necessary performance for large installations, provided that they are properly configured and that enough bandwidth is allocated to the instances.</li><li>It is recommended that you stay away from Windows deployments. There is no freely available Cassandra Windows installer.</li><li>If we look at cloud hosting, the older instances in Amazon use the Xen hypervisor (xcp), whereas the new ones use the kvm hypervisor. These are the instances you should use: preferably, the NVMe-based i3 instances, next would be r5, and finally m5. What is critical is to provision the filesystem properly.</li><li>On the client side, the same issues of resources over-allocation can be a performance killer. Thin provisioning affects performance because disk caches are used extensively. Therefore, what is more, important when using VDI's is working on machines that have faster processors. When provisioning the VDI's, we do not recommend that you allocate a lot of CPU's because they will not be used. It is important to have the fastest processors.</li></ul><h3>Antivirus</h3><p>Since some antivirus software can cause a significant performance decrease upon opening a project and performing other actions, we recommend that you exclude the modeling tool's home directory from the antivirus scanning scope.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679098 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00563: Hide Metatype

- page_id: `169679098`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679098/Hide+Metatype
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-08-16T11:49:48.238+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Creating Customization Data > Customization Elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Indicates whether the customized stereotype is recognized as a new type. If the **Hide Metatype** property value is *false*, the DSL element is *not* considered as a new type element and will not be displayed in the shortcut menus (see the **Possible Owners** property).

To define the hide metatype property

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing Specification Window' space='MD2024xR1']**Customization** Specification window, click the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customization Target' space='MD2024xR1']**Customization Target** property and then select a customization target.
2. Set the **Hide Metatype** property to *true*.
3. Reopen the project.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Customization Data' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Customization Data' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customization Elements' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customization Element Properties Description' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customization Target' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating your First Customization' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and specifying derived properties' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining expressions' space='MD2024xR1']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675869 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00564: Hide Uncommon Elements filter

- page_id: `169675869`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675869/Hide+Uncommon+Elements+filter
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-06-17T12:01:19.991+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Selecting elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1827084503

1827084505

1827084504

You can enable the *Hide Uncommon Elements* filter in the *Select Type* dialog to leave out uncommon element types and thus find the ones you need easier.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Hide Uncommon Elements filter in the Select Type dialog

Once *Hide Uncommon Elements*is enabled, the**filter returns only those elements whose types are based on:

- Class (except Metaclass and Term)
- Component
- Signal
- Interface
- DataType
- Enumeration
- PrimitiveType
- AssociationClass

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Specified property type

If the SysML profile is loaded, only elements of*Block*and*ValueType*types are listed in the *Select Type* dialog.

If the Stereotype tag types are being specified, metaclasses are not filtered out.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Metaclasses in the *Select Type* dialog

1827084502

**Related Pages:**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting port and part types' space='MD2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1ec78cb1-1b33-485a-9ae2-06da7651d33a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1827084503</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4a58628e-2ebc-4314-b83b-788415cdec9c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1827084505</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8f52f189-903c-41ab-a585-197b4740bd5b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1827084504</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p style="text-align: left;">You can enable the <em>Hide Uncommon Elements</em> filter in the <em>Select Type</em> dialog to leave out uncommon element types and thus find the ones you need easier. </p><p style="text-align: left;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="selecting_type_with_hide_uncommon_elements_filter_applied.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Hide Uncommon Elements filter" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"> Hide Uncommon Elements filter in the Select Type dialog</h6><p style="text-align: left;">Once <em style="color: rgb(23,43,77);font-size: 14.0px;font-weight: 400;letter-spacing: 0.0px;text-align: left;">Hide Uncommon Elements</em><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"> is enabled, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="ad9fba9c-7ee0-4224-929e-b2f1b0fec59f">the</ac:inline-comment-marker></span><em style="color: rgb(23,43,77);font-size: 14.0px;font-weight: 400;letter-spacing: 0.0px;text-align: left;"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="ad9fba9c-7ee0-4224-929e-b2f1b0fec59f"> </ac:inline-comment-marker></em><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="ad9fba9c-7ee0-4224-929e-b2f1b0fec59f">filter returns only those elements whose types are based on:</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><ul><li><p>Class (except Metaclass and Term)</p></li><li><p>Component</p></li><li><p>Signal</p></li><li><p>Interface</p></li><li><p>DataType</p></li><li><p>Enumeration</p></li><li><p>PrimitiveType</p></li><li><p>AssociationClass</p></li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification_of_property.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Hide Uncommon Elements filter" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">Specified property type</span></h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c1621df7-fe51-4216-8de4-b51821864431"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the SysML profile is loaded, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="01a1bfa5-6266-4990-9bce-4cd4651dcd91">only elements of </ac:inline-comment-marker><em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="01a1bfa5-6266-4990-9bce-4cd4651dcd91">Block</ac:inline-comment-marker></em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="01a1bfa5-6266-4990-9bce-4cd4651dcd91"> and </ac:inline-comment-marker><em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="01a1bfa5-6266-4990-9bce-4cd4651dcd91">ValueType </ac:inline-comment-marker></em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="01a1bfa5-6266-4990-9bce-4cd4651dcd91">types</ac:inline-comment-marker> are listed in the <em>Select Type</em> dialog.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5b8bd827-e025-4399-a512-4fc62f7e78f4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the Stereotype tag types are being specified, metaclasses are not filtered out.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select_type_dialog_stereotype.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Hide Uncommon Elements filter" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Metaclasses in the <em>Select Type</em> dialog</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f192fffe-6da8-4c1e-9cf5-6e54286fa9be"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1827084502</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages:</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Selecting port and part types" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````
