<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676892 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00620: Inspecting differences in the Change details panel

- page_id: `169676892`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676892/Inspecting+differences+in+the+Change+details+panel
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2019-06-05T14:30:49.530+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Comparing projects > Analyzing comparison results
- labels: ['change-details', 'differences', 'difference-viewer']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **Change details** panel displays a tree structure reflecting the differences between the two versions of either the element selected in the **Differences** tree or the property selected in the **Specification** panel. If several elements or properties are selected, then all changes that have been detected in these elements or properties accordingly will be displayed in the **Change** **details** panel. The panel is located above the **Specification** panel on the **Difference Viewer** window.

The panel contains a tree structure that has 1 top-level branch – Changes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

In this panel, you can see dependent changes. In order to see them, expand the **Dependent changes** branch under an element with dependent changes.

For the dependent changes description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space='MD2024xR1'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The <strong>Change details</strong> panel displays a tree structure reflecting the differences between the two versions of either the element selected in the <strong>Differences</strong> tree or the property selected in the <strong>Specification</strong> panel. If several elements or properties are selected, then all changes that have been detected in these elements or properties accordingly will be displayed in the <strong>Change</strong> <strong>details</strong> panel. The panel is located above the <strong>Specification</strong> panel on the <strong>Difference Viewer</strong> window.</p><p>The panel contains a tree structure that has 1 top-level branch <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="edfa489e-d703-4e81-84d4-4e561ff6619a">– Changes.</ac:inline-comment-marker>   <span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> <br /> </span></p><p><ac:image ac:title="Change details dialog" ac:alt="Change details dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="change_details_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Inspecting differences in the Change details panel" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>In this panel, you can see dependent changes. In order to see them, expand the <strong>Dependent changes</strong> branch under an element with dependent changes.</p><p>For the dependent changes description, r<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="319a5311-84d4-4531-8f99-20cc6ed2e97e">efer t</ac:inline-comment-marker>o <ac:link ac:anchor="dependentchange"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Understanding change types" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Dependent changes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676888 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00621: Inspecting element property changes in the differences Specification panel

- page_id: `169676888`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676888/Inspecting+element+property+changes+in+the+differences+Specification+panel
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2015-12-14T12:51:58.617+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Comparing projects > Analyzing comparison results
- labels: ['property-changes', 'compare-specification-panel']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can see all the details of the element property changes in the **Specification** panel that is located at the bottom of the **Difference Viewer** window. All you need to do is selecting the appropriate element in the **Differences** tree.

As you can see in the following figure, the first column of the panel contains a list of element property names, and other columns display their value changes that have been detected in both base and compared projects.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To navigate from the **Specification** panel to the **Differences** tree, do either

- In the Properties column, right-click the name of a property, which references other elements. Then on its shortcut menu, point to Select in Differences tree and choose an element to which you want to navigate.
- I n the Base or Compared column, right-click the value of a property, which references other elements. Then on its shortcut menu choose an element to which you want to navigate.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 **Inspecting differences between textual values**

You can see exact changes between textual values of element properties, such as element documentation, comments, pre- and post- conditions of a use case. Differences of compared texts can be displayed in a single dialog, where inserted and deleted parts of the text are appropriately highlighted. As of version 18.3, the differences between compared text written in HTML are now represented as rich text, enabling you to see all changes in formatting as well.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 To see the differences of compared texts, do either

- Select a property with a modified text on the Specification panel and then click [ATTACHMENT filename='compare_property_values_button.png'] on the toolbar.
- Double-click the property on the Specification panel. The Compare property dialog opens.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676057 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00622: Inspecting element property changes in the Specification panel

- page_id: `169676057`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676057/Inspecting+element+property+changes+in+the+Specification+panel
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:34:19.745+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools > Model Merge > Analyzing and managing merge results
- labels: ['merge-specification-panel', 'property-changes']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1086956613

1086956615

1086956614

You can see all the details of the element property changes in the **Specification** panel that is located at the top right of the **Merge** window. All you need to do is selecting the element in the **Merged Result** tree or list.

As you can see in the following figure, the first column of the panel contains a list of element property names, and other columns display their value changes that have been detected in both source and the and target. Third column Merge Results Preview shows the final merge result. Properties changes can be accepted or rejected in this panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To navigate from the **Specification** panel to the **Merged Result** tree, do either

- In the Properties column, right-click the name of a property, which references other elements. Then on its shortcut menu, point to Select in Merged Result Tree and choose an element to which you want to navigate.
- In the Source , Ancestor , Target or Merge Result Preview column, right-click the value of a property, which references other elements. Then on its shortcut menu choose an element to which you want to navigate.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The **Ancestor** column by default is hidden. To show it in the **Specification**panel, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the **Merge**toolbar and select **Show Ancestor**.

The Multiplicity attribute is merged as a string, meaning that Lower Value and Upper Value are merged and transformed into numerical/text types accordingly.

**Inspecting differences between textual values**

You can see exact changes between textual values of element properties, such as element documentation, comments, pre- and post-conditions of a use case. Differences of compared texts can be displayed in a single dialog, where inserted and deleted parts of the text are highlighted.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
To see the differences of compared texts, do either

- Select a property with a modified text on the Specification panel and then click [ATTACHMENT filename='compare_property_values_button.png'] on the toolbar.
- Double-click the property on the Specification panel. The Compare property dialog opens.

1086956612

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678249 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00623: Installation

- page_id: `169678249`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678249/Installation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2022-04-26T11:59:48.688+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Report Wizard template editor
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To install Report Wizard Template Editor in Microsoft Word

1. Copy all files and subdirectories from the <install.dir>\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.reportwizard\vba folder to %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP**** (%APPDATA% is the location where user data is stored). For example,
  - On Windows 2000 and Windows XP: C:\Documents and Settings\User Name\Application Data\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP
  - On Windows Vista: C:\Users\UserName\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP
  - On Windows 7, 8, and later: C:\Users\UserName\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP
2. Restart Microsoft Word.

Microsoft Word 64bit may require mscomctl.ocx for this add-in. To install mscomctl.ocx:

1. Go to [https://www.microsoft.com/en-au/download/details.aspx?id=10019](https://www.microsoft.com/en-au/download/details.aspx?id=10019)

2. Download Microsoft Visual Basic 6.0 Common Controls

3. Install mscomctl.ocx by extracting VisualBasic6-????.exe and then copy mscomctl.ocx to C:\Windows\System32 or C:\Windows\SysWOW64

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To install Report Wizard Template Editor in Microsoft Word</p><hr /><ol><li>Copy all files and subdirectories from the <em>&lt;install.dir&gt;\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.reportwizard\vba</em> folder to <em>%APPDATA%\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP<strong> </strong></em>(%APPDATA% is the location where user data is stored). For example,<br /><ul><li>On Windows 2000 and Windows XP: <em>C:\Documents and Settings\User Name\Application Data\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP</em></li><li>On Windows Vista: <em>C:\Users\UserName\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP</em></li><li>On Windows 7, 8, and later: <em>C:\Users\UserName\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP</em></li></ul></li><li>Restart Microsoft Word.<br /><br /></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="edbea50e-de80-4591-a358-d537c22b71e9"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Microsoft Word 64bit may require mscomctl.ocx for this add-in. To install mscomctl.ocx:</p><p>1. Go to <a href="https://www.microsoft.com/en-au/download/details.aspx?id=10019">https://www.microsoft.com/en-au/download/details.aspx?id=10019</a></p><p>2. Download Microsoft Visual Basic 6.0 Common Controls</p><p>3. Install mscomctl.ocx by extracting VisualBasic6-????.exe and then copy mscomctl.ocx to C:\Windows\System32 or C:\Windows\SysWOW64</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678795 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00625: Installing custom tool

- page_id: `169678795`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678795/Installing+custom+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-12-26T10:26:06.681+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix A: report extensions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To install the extension, copy the *JAR* file containing a custom tool class into the extensions directory under the Report Wizard plugin directory or template directory. Report Wizard will automatically load a new *JAR* file when a new report is generated.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To install the extension, copy the <em>JAR</em> file containing a custom tool class into the extensions directory under the Report Wizard plugin directory or template directory. Report Wizard will automatically load a new <em>JAR</em> file when a new report is generated.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675175 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00626: Instance Specification

- page_id: `169675175`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675175/Instance+Specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T17:52:34.442+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: ['slot', 'slot-specification']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

72524162

72524165

116896173

**On this page**

4

72524164

INLINE

##### Introduction

An instance specification specifies the existence of an entity in a modeled system and completely or partiallydescribes the entity.

The description may include:

- The classification of an entity by one or more classifiers of which the entity is an instance. If the only classifier specified is abstract, then the instance specification only partially describes the entity.
- A kind of instance based on its classifier or classifiers. For example, an instance specification whose classifier is a class describes an object of that class, while an instance specification whose classifier is an association describes a link of that association.
- A specification of structural features values of the entity. Not all structural features of all classifiers of the instance specification need to be represented by slots, in which case the instance specification is a partial description.
- A specification of how to compute, derive, or construct the instance (optional).

Modeling tool allows you to create the instances of classifiers such as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MD2024xR1'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface' space='MD2024xR1'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Enumeration' space='MD2024xR1'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case' space='MD2024xR1'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actor' space='MD2024xR1'],[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Node' space='MD2024xR1'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component' space='MD2024xR1'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Manifestations' space='MD2024xR1'].

In the Component (or Deployment) diagram Node Instance, Component Instance, Artifact Instance elements are the same Instance Specification elements with an assigned component, node or artifact.

The instances are shown using a rectangle by underlining the name string of an instance element. The instance of an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actor' space='MD2024xR1']is shown as an actor “stick man” figure with the actor’s name below the symbol.

You can specify instance properties in the instance [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1']. In the same window, you can find a description of each property. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the Specification window. If you need to handle a huge amount of instance specifications, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instance table' space='MD2024xR1'].

##### Assigning a classifier to an Instance Specification

To assign a classifier to an instance, do one of the following

- On the diagram, click an instance shape, and then on the smart manipulator toolbar, click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. In the open list, select the classifier.
- On the diagram, click an instance shape, and then press **Ctrl+T**. In the open list, select the classifier.
- On the diagram, click an instance shape, then click the name area. Type the “:” and then type the classifier name. Press **Enter**or click anywhere on the diagram. The classifier is created and assigned. Type the “:” colon, then press Ctrl+Space or Ctrl+Backspace and then in the open list, select the classifier.
- In the Containment tree, select a classifier and drag it to the instance shape.

Use the Node Instance button to create the Instance Specification withassigned Node and the Node Instance will have a Node shape.

##### Assigning Instance Specification as Default Value

To assign Instance Specification as Default Value

- Drag Instance Specification element on Classifier property on a diagram.

Drag-and-drop feature is available only if Instance Specification classifiers are compatible with the Property type and ifProperty is editable.

Setting the initial value to an attribute of the assigned classifier

To set the initial value to an attribute of the assigned classifier

1. Open the Instance Specification window.
2. In the **Slots**property group, select a property and c lick the **Create Value**button.
3. Type the needed value in the field on the right.

##### Showing slots of the assigned classifier

To show slots of the assigned classifier

1. Select an instance shape and click the Compartments button [IMAGE alt='' src=''].
2. In the menu, select **Slots**.

By default slots of the classifier are suppressed.

##### Displaying slot type on the Instance Specification symbol

To display slot type on the instance symbol

1. Open instance **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024xR1']** dialog.
2. Set the **Show Slot Type**property to one of the following:

- None
- Name
- Qualified name

##### Displaying specification value on the Instance Specification symbol

To display specification value on the Instance Specification symbol

1. Open instance [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024xR1'] dialog.
2. Select the **Show Specification Value** check box.

##### Saving Instance values to the default property values of the Class (Block)

When you have a Class (Block) and an Instance of that Class (Block)which contains some particular values, you can save those values as default to the Values property of the Class (Block).

To save the Instance values as default

1. Do one of the following:
  - On the diagram pane, select an Instance.
  - In the Containment tree, select an Instance.
  - In the Instance table, select an Instance.
2. Right-click an Instance and from the shortcut menu, select Tools > Save to Default Value(s) .
3. In the open dialog, select the needed options:
  - **Save to composite parts recursively**.
  - **Redefine inherited properties**.
  - Save derived property values . [ATTACHMENT filename='save_to_default_values.png']
4. Click OK .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of Instance property values saved as default in the Block value properties. The "Save to composite parts recursively" option was selected.

72524160

## PAGE 00628: Instance table

- page_id: `169674441`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169674441/Instance+table

- version_date: `2024-02-01T16:47:51.198+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

221798943

221798946

221798941

**On this page**

221798945

##### **Description**

This feature is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.

The Instance Table is one of four tables which allow you to manage Instance Specifications of your model in a spreadsheet-like form. Each row in the table represents an instance.****

##### **Purpose**

An Instance Table allows you to:

- Create Instance Specifications (instances) for the classifiers.
- Review the instances of one or more of the classifiers in a single place.
- Edit Slot values of the instances displayed in the table.
- Customize the representation of the table.
- Export the data into .html , .csv, or * .xlsx files.

##### **Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### **Specific tasks in the Instance Table**

The following outlines the specific procedures when working with the Instance Table:

## PAGE 00630: Interaction

- page_id: `169675183`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675183/Interaction
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-04-03T12:38:11.268+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Interaction is a unit of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Behavior' space='MD2024xR1'] focusing on the observable exchange of information between connectable elements, a specialization of Interaction Fragment and of Behavior.

An Interaction originates in a modeling tool while creatingCommunicationandSequencediagrams. When you double click on an Interaction, a diagram is opened. 
In theelementdialog box, you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it. For more information about element dialog boxes and panes of the element dialog boxes,click here. 
 
To open the**Interaction**dialog box, choose the**Specification**command from the Interaction shortcut menu. 
 
In the**Interaction**dialog box, the**Messages**pane lists Messages contained in this Interaction.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

When you double click on an Interaction, a diagram is opened. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In the </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">element</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> dialog box, you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it. For more information about element dialog boxes and panes of the element dialog boxes, </span><a>click here</a><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">  </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">To open the </span><span class="hcp1" style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><strong>Interaction</strong></span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> dialog box, choose the </span><strong>Specification</strong><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> command from the Interaction shortcut menu.   </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">  </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In the </span><span class="hcp1" style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><strong>Interaction</strong></span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> dialog box, the </span><span class="hcp1" style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><strong>Messages</strong></span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> pane lists Messages contained in this Interaction.</span></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675184 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00631: Interaction operand

- page_id: `169675184`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675184/Interaction+operand
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-03-29T14:46:18.509+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Interaction
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Interaction operand represents an Interaction with an optional guard expression. The Interaction operand is executed only if the guard expression tests true at runtime. The object that starts the interaction requires all the information necessary to make the decision about whether to proceed. The absence of any guard condition means that the interaction is always executed. 
In the Interaction operand [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'], you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">An Interaction operand represents an Interaction with an optional guard expression. The Interaction operand is executed only if the guard expression tests true at runtime. The object that starts the interaction requires all the information necessary to make the decision about whether to proceed. The absence of any guard condition means that the interaction is always executed. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In the <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Interaction operand <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link></span></span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">, you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">  </span></p>
````

## PAGE 00632: Interaction Overview diagram

- page_id: `169673934`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673934/Interaction+Overview+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2022-01-27T14:44:43.261+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > UML diagrams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

308856586

308856588

308856587

**Overview**

An Interaction Overview diagram is a type of Interaction diagram, which falls under the behavioral diagramming family.An Interaction Overview diagram focuses on the flow of control between interactions. It is based on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024xR1'] notation. Interactions in the Interaction Overview diagram are represented using the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction Use' space='MD2024xR1'] element. All elements in the diagram, except for the Interaction Use, are the same as in the Activity diagram.

**Purpose**

An Interaction Overview diagram is intended to be used to model the flow of logic in a series of interactions. To put it another way, the Interaction Overview diagram uses blocks of interactions and individual interactions instead of modeling a series of discrete behaviors (activities).

**Usage**

Interaction Overview diagrams visualize a sequence of activities just like Activity diagrams do; however, they picture each individual entry as a frame which can contain a nested interaction diagram.

**Summary**

Interaction Overview diagrams are valuable because they:

- provide a high-level view of the logical progression through a set of interactions
- keep the focus on the "big picture" and away from implementation details
- encourage the reuse of interactions

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of an Interaction Overview diagram

308856585

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='MD2024xR1']

It is based on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /></ac:link> notation. Interactions in the Interaction Overview diagram are represented using the<ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interaction Use" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ Interaction Use]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> element. All elements in the diagram, except for the Interaction Use, are the same as in the Activity diagram.</p><p><strong>Purpose</strong></p><p>An Interaction Overview diagram is intended to be used to model the flow of logic in a series of interactions. To put it another way, the Interaction Overview diagram uses blocks of interactions and individual interactions instead of modeling a series of discrete behaviors (activities). </p><p><strong>Usage</strong></p><p>Interaction Overview diagrams visualize a sequence of activities just like Activity diagrams do; however, they picture each individual entry as a frame which can contain a nested interaction diagram. </p><p><strong>Summary</strong></p><p>Interaction Overview diagrams are valuable because they:</p><ul><li>provide a high-level view of the logical progression through a set of interactions</li><li>keep the focus on the &quot;big picture&quot; and away from implementation details</li><li>encourage the reuse of interactions </li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="interaction_overview_diagram_example.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interaction Overview diagram" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of an Interaction Overview diagram<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e2a23b37-52a4-4422-9417-7709aa16f0f8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308856585</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00633: Interaction Overview diagram elements

- page_id: `169673544`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673544/Interaction+Overview+diagram+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-12-28T10:18:22.771+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Importing data from other UML tools and formats > Enterprise Architect Import > Special Transformation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

560251696

560251698

560251694

**On this page**

3

560251697

The following sections describe additional EA specific Interaction Overview diagram information.

##### Interaction Overview Diagram

An [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction Overview diagram' space='MD2024xR1'] is one of four types of Interaction diagrams. The other three are [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Time diagram' space='MD2024xR1'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD2024xR1'], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Communication diagram' space='MD2024xR1']diagrams. An Interaction Overview diagram, just like an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024xR1'], visualizes a sequence of activities. Most of the notation elements for Interaction Overview diagrams are the same as those for Activity diagrams (such as Initial, Decision, Fork, Join, and Final [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Node' space='MD2024xR1']). Interaction Occurrences and Interaction elements are two new elements in the interaction overview diagrams.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Interaction Overview Diagram.

Afterconversion, the Interaction Overview diagram will be placed within anActivityelement.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Interaction Overview Diagram Placement.

###### Interaction Element

Interaction elements display an inline Interaction diagram (Interaction Overview, Timing, Sequence, or Communication diagram). In EA, Interaction elements can be created to display other diagrams than those classified as Interaction diagrams.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Interaction element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Interaction Element in Interaction Overview Diagram.

###### Interaction Occurrence

Interaction Occurrence elements refer to an existing Interaction diagram. They are visually represented by a rectangular frame, which can be created by dragging the Interaction diagram from the project Browser to an Interaction Overview diagram and selecting the **Interaction Occurrence** command from the shortcut menu.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Interaction Occurence element.

560258099

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction Overview diagram' space='MD2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The other three are <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Time diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Timing]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Sequence]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Communication diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Communication ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>diagrams.  An Interaction Overview diagram, just like an <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /></ac:link>, visualizes a sequence of activities. Most of the notation elements for Interaction Overview diagrams are the same as those for Activity diagrams (such as Initial, Decision, Fork, Join, and Final <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Node" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Nodes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>). Interaction Occurrences and Interaction elements are two new elements in the interaction overview diagrams.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="interactionOverview.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interaction Overview diagram elements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Interaction Overview Diagram.</h6><p><span>After<span> </span>conversion, the Interaction Overview diagram will be placed within an<span> </span>Activity<span> </span>element.</span></p><p><span><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="interactionOverview_placement.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interaction Overview diagram elements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Interaction Overview Diagram Placement.</h6><h4>Interaction Element</h4><p>Interaction elements display an inline Interaction diagram (Interaction Overview, Timing, Sequence, or Communication diagram). In EA, Interaction elements can be created to display other diagrams than those classified as Interaction diagrams.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="interactionElement.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interaction Overview diagram elements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Interaction element.</h6><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="interactionElement_inOverview.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interaction Overview diagram elements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Interaction Element in Interaction Overview Diagram.</h6><h4>Interaction Occurrence</h4><p>Interaction Occurrence elements refer to an existing Interaction diagram. They are visually represented by a rectangular frame, which can be created by dragging the Interaction diagram from the project Browser to an Interaction Overview diagram and selecting the <strong>Interaction Occurrence</strong> command from the shortcut menu.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="interactionOccurenceElement.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interaction Overview diagram elements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Interaction Occurence element.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3aa25730-3ab6-469e-a903-3fe1d530cf3f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">560258099</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interaction Overview diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675185 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00634: Interaction Use

- page_id: `169675185`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675185/Interaction+Use
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-09T05:00:34.423+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Interaction
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Interactions are units of behavior of an enclosing Classifier. They focus on the passing of information with Messages between the Connectable Elements of the Classifier.

A reference to the interaction can be created.

You can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing property values' space='MD2024xR1'] in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1']. In the same window, you can find the description of each property. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1']Specification window.

To add a reference to an interaction use, do one of the following

- Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'] of the interaction use and click the cell of the **Refers To**property value. Then click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] and in the open dialog, select the element. Click **OK**when you are done.
- On the diagram, right-click the interaction use shape, and then point to **Refers To**. Then select an element you wish to refere to.
- On the diagram, click an interaction use shape, and then press **Ctrl+T**. Then select an element you wish to refere to.
- On the diagram, click an interaction use shape, and then on the smart manipulator toolbar, click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. Then select an element you wish to refere to.
- In the Containment tree, select an element and drag it to the interaction use shape.

To add an actual [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Gate' space='MD2024xR1']

1. Add a reference to the diagram, from which frame the formal message is created.
2. Draw an actual message with the selected formal [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Gate' space='MD2024xR1'] to the current interaction use.

The rake icon[IMAGE alt='' src='']is displayed on the shape if the interaction use refers to another sequence diagram. The rake icon is not shown by default therefore you need to set the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying rake icon' space='MD2024xR1']property value to true in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024xR1']dialog.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case diagram' space='MD2024xR1']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024xR1']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675188 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00635: Interface

- page_id: `169675188`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675188/Interface
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-09T05:01:00.987+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An interface is a specifier for the externally-visible operations of a class, component, or other classifiers (including subsystems) without a specification of the internal structure. Each interface often specifies only a limited part of the behavior of an actual class.

The set of interfaces realized by a classifier is its provided interfaces, which represent the obligations that instances of that classifier have to their clients. They describe the services that the instances of that classifier offer to their clients.

You can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing property values' space='MD2024xR1'] in the interface [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1']. In the same window, you can find the description of each property. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'].

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

true

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case diagram' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024xR1']Activity diagram

Each interface often specifies only a limited part of the behavior of an actual class.</p><p>The set of interfaces realized by a classifier is its provided interfaces, which represent the obligations that instances of that classifier have to their clients. They describe the services that the instances of that classifier offer to their clients.</p><p>You can <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Editing property values" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[specify interface properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in the interface <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675197 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00636: Interface realization

- page_id: `169675197`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675197/Interface+realization
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-04-03T12:40:25.068+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Interface realization is a specialized [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Realization' space='MD2024xR1'] relationship between a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Classifier Behavior property' space='CST190'] and an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface' space='MD2024xR1'], signifying that the realizing classifier conforms to the contract specified by the Interface.

A classifier that implements an interface specifies instances conforming to the interface and its ancestors. A classifier may implement a number of interfaces.

In the Interface realization [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'], you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it. 
 
To open theInterfaceRealizationdialog box, choose theSpecificationcommand from the Realization shortcut menu. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

In the Interface Realization [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'], the Implementing Classifier property references the classifier that realizes the Interface to which it points.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An Interface realization is a specialized <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Realization" /></ac:link> relationship between a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST190" ri:content-title="Classifier Behavior property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Classifier Behavior]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and an <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interface" /></ac:link>, signifying that the realizing classifier conforms to the contract specified by the Interface.</p><p>A classifier that implements an interface specifies instances conforming to the interface and its ancestors. A classifier may implement a number of interfaces.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> In the Interface realization <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link></span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">, you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">  </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">To open the </span><span class="hcp1" style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Interface</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> </span><span class="hcp1" style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Realization</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> dialog box, choose the </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Specification</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> command from the Realization shortcut menu. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Interface_realization.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interface realization" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </span></p><p>In the <span class="hcp1">Interface</span> <span class="hcp1">Realization</span> <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>, the <span class="hcp1">Implementing</span> <span class="hcp1">Classifier</span> property references the classifier that realizes the Interface to which it points.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675199 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00637: Internal Transition

- page_id: `169675199`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675199/Internal+Transition
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-07-07T20:48:21.387+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In all other cases, the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Action' space='MD2024xR1'] label identifies the event that triggered the corresponding Action expression. These events are called Internal Transitions and are semantically equivalent to self transitions except that the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State' space='MD2024xR1'] is neither exited nor re-entered. This means that the corresponding exit and entry actions are not performed.

#### TIP: Fork And Join

Fork And Join

For more information about defining transitions, read [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Fork and Join' space='MD2024xR1'].

Specify the Internal Transition in the Transition's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'].

#### TIP: Fork And Join

Fork And Join

For a more detailed description of this dialog, read [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space='MD2024xR1'].

To create and specify an Internal Transition

In your project, if you have a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram' space='MD2024xR1'] created, do the following:

1. Double-click the State or select Specification from the State's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Shortcut menu' space='CDH185'] . The State's Specification Window opens.
2. Click the Internal Transitions group.
3. Click the Create button. The Transition's Specification window opens.
4. Specify an Internal Transition .
5. Click Close.

To remove an Internal Transition

1. Double-click the State or select Specification from the State's shortcut menu. The State's Specification Window opens.
2. Click the Internal Transitions group.
3. The Transition's Specification window opens.
4. Click Delete . Then, Click Close .

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Formatting symbols' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Action' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Event' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Fork and Join' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space='MD2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>In all other cases, the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Action" /></ac:link> label identifies the event that triggered the corresponding Action expression. These events are called Internal Transitions and are semantically equivalent to self transitions except that the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="State" /></ac:link> is neither exited nor re-entered. This means that the corresponding exit and entry actions are not performed. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e9018626-7053-43bd-ae53-c4a4ece9de77"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Fork And Join</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>For more information about defining transitions, read <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Fork and Join" /></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">Specify the Internal Transition in the Transition's <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b2ddc42b-1ffe-4fd2-abaf-50081e196d4d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Fork And Join</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>For a more detailed description of this dialog, read <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Transition" /></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To create and specify an Internal Transition</p><hr /><p>In your project, if you have a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="State Machine diagram" /></ac:link> created, do the following:</p><ol><li>Double-click the State or select Specification from the State's <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CDH185" ri:content-title="Shortcut menu" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[shortcut menu]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. The State's Specification Window opens.</li><li>Click the<strong> Internal Transitions </strong>group. </li><li>Click the <strong>Create </strong>button. The Transition's Specification window opens. </li><li>Specify an <strong>Internal Transition</strong>.</li><li>Click Close.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To remove an Internal Transition</p><hr /><ol><li>Double-click the State or select Specification from the State's shortcut menu. The State's Specification Window opens.</li><li>Click the <strong>Internal Transitions</strong> group. </li><li>The Transition's Specification window opens.</li><li>Click <strong>Delete</strong>. Then, Click <strong>Close</strong>.</li></ol><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ff1aef2b-4429-4784-bb8f-aa49ccc1bf16"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related Pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Formatting symbols" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Action" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Event" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="State Machine diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Fork and Join" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Transition" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675200 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00638: Interruptible activity region

- page_id: `169675200`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675200/Interruptible+activity+region
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-05-29T07:23:16.101+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Interruptable region contains activity nodes. When a token leaves an interruptable region via edges designated by the region as interrupting edges, all tokens and behaviours in the region are terminated.

Specify the Interruptible Activity Region in the Interruptible Activity Region [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'].

To make an edge as interrupting, simply select an edge and click the "lightning" button, as shown in the image bellow.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Mark edge as interrupting

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An Interruptable region contains activity nodes. When a token leaves an interruptable region via edges designated by the region as interrupting edges, all tokens and behaviours in the region are terminated.</p><p>Specify the Interruptible Activity Region in the Interruptible Activity Region <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>.</p><p>To make an edge as interrupting, simply select an edge and click the &quot;lightning&quot; button, as shown in the image bellow.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="make_interrupting.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interruptible activity region" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>Mark edge as interrupting</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679544 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00639: Introducing Macro Engine

- page_id: `169679544`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679544/Introducing+Macro+Engine
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:30.755+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Macro Engine (previously called Script Engine) allows you to create your own macro (script) by using BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript Rhino, or Jython. With Macro Engine, you can control everything that is allowed in Open API, for example, transforming and manipulating models. Learn more about Open API in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Developer Guides Home' space='DEVG2024xR1'] and find sample macros in *<modeling tool installation directory>/samples/product features/macros*.

Macro Engine comes with Professional, Architect, and Enterprise Editions starting from version 16.6 and later.

##### Changing Javascript Nashorn to Javascript Rhino

The Macro Engine supports Javascript Rhino. If you have any macros written in Javascript Nashorn, which is [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn' space='MD2024xR1'], you can change it to Javascript Rhino.

To change to Javascript Rhino

- Click Tools > Macros > Change JS Nashorn to Rhino

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Macro Engine (previously called Script Engine) allows you to create your own macro (script) by using BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript Rhino, or Jython. With Macro Engine, you can control everything that is allowed in Open API, for example, transforming and manipulating models. Learn more about Open API in <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="DEVG2024xR1" ri:content-title="Developer Guides Home" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Developer Guide]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and find sample macros in <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;/samples/product features/macros</em>.</p><p>Macro Engine comes with Professional, Architect, and Enterprise Editions starting from version 16.6 and later.</p><h3>Changing Javascript Nashorn to Javascript Rhino</h3><p>The Macro Engine supports Javascript Rhino. If you have any macros written in Javascript Nashorn, which is <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[deprecated]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, you can change it to Javascript Rhino.</p><p>To change to Javascript Rhino</p><hr /><ul><li>Click <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Macros</strong> &gt; <strong>Change JS Nashorn to Rhino</strong></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678703 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00640: Introduction

- page_id: `169678703`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678703/Introduction
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T16:21:48.029+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1355068225

1355068226

1355068226

This user guide demonstrates how to create a report template using Report Wizard and use it in the modeling tool. To make the most of Report Wizard, you need to understand Apache Velocity and the modeling tool elements. Report Wizard uses Apache Velocity, this means that you will need to know how to create a Velocity template and that every rule that applies to Velocity will also apply to the Report Wizard.

Velocity is a Java-based template engine that processes templates and references Java objects to produce output documents. A basic Velocity template can contain static text, layouts, conditional statements, and placeholders for each referenced Java object. When a template is being read by Velocity, conditional statements will be processed and placeholders will be replaced with the value from the referenced Java objects. You can use Velocity, for example, to generate web pages, emails, and read *XML* documents, but with Report Wizard it is now possible to use Velocity to read other types of templates, such as Rich Text Format documents. This user guide covers some basic aspects of Velocity, modeling tool elements, and Report Wizard directives.

1355068224

**Related page**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9c5c469b-c11c-4cff-be12-1bba3a457112"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1355068225</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4ab531a4-acde-4318-b5c0-529b8d53f6b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1355068226</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7a32d2eb-d69f-4be1-97c8-bafb80e2249b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1355068226</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This user guide demonstrates how to create a report template using Report Wizard and use it in the modeling tool. To make the most of Report Wizard, you need to understand Apache Velocity and the modeling tool elements. Report Wizard uses Apache Velocity, this means that you will need to know how to create a Velocity template and that every rule that applies to Velocity will also apply to the Report Wizard.</p><p>Velocity is a Java-based template engine that processes templates and references Java objects to produce output documents. A basic Velocity template can contain static text, layouts, conditional statements, and placeholders for each referenced Java object. When a template is being read by Velocity, conditional statements will be processed and placeholders will be replaced with the value from the referenced Java objects. You can use Velocity, for example, to generate web pages, emails, and read <em>XML</em> documents, but with Report Wizard it is now possible to use Velocity to read other types of templates, such as Rich Text Format documents. This user guide covers some basic aspects of Velocity, modeling tool elements, and Report Wizard directives.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2794887a-6f98-48f2-9b85-a3f47c2851b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1355068224</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="4dc280e8-5e54-4745-a5b5-4e96822291c6" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673090 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00641: Invalid Connector

- page_id: `169673090`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673090/Invalid+Connector
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-10T11:09:35.535+02:00`
- ancestors: UML correctness rules (in progress)
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

InvConnector

**Description**

This validation rule checks if the Connector's owner is wrong or if the Connector Ends specified in the model are changed.

**Severity**

error

**Constrained Element**

Connector, Connector End, Diagram

**Solvers**

- Update Connector Ends - updates the Connector Ends to the correct ones and/or moves the Connector to the correct owner.
- Delete from Diagram - deletes the Connector from the Diagram.
- Delete from Model - deletes the Connector from the Model.

**Example**

###### **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**Part Properties typed by *VCCU* and *Cabin*are under the owner Block *Vehicle in Use*. The Connector between these Part Properties is under a different owner *Vehicle in Maintenance*, which causes an error. 
The error is resolved via the **Update Connector Ends**solver, which moves the Connector under the Block *Vehicle in Use*, making it the owner.****

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>InvConnector</p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if the <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Connector's owner is wrong or if the Connector Ends specified in the model are changed.</span></p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>error</p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Connector, Connector End, Diagram</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><ul><li><strong>Update Connector Ends</strong> - updates the Connector Ends to the correct ones and/or moves the Connector to the correct owner. </li><li><strong>Delete from Diagram </strong>- deletes the Connector from the Diagram.</li><li><strong>Delete from Model </strong>- deletes the Connector from the Model.</li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><strong><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Invalid Connector.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Invalid Connector" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong>Part Properties typed by <em>VCCU</em> and <em>Cabin </em>are under the owner Block <em>Vehicle in Use</em>. The Connector between these Part Properties is under a different owner <em>Vehicle in Maintenance</em>, which causes an error.<br />The error is resolved via the <strong>Update Connector Ends </strong>solver, which moves the Connector under the Block <em>Vehicle in Use</em>, making it the owner.<strong> </strong></h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673125 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00642: Invalid Connector (Symbols)

- page_id: `169673125`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673125/Invalid+Connector+Symbols
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-06-02T12:28:27.707+02:00`
- ancestors: UML correctness constraints (in progress)
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

InvConnector

**Description**

This validation rule checks if the Connector's owner is wrong or if the Connector's ends specified in the model are changed.

**Severity**

error

**Constrained Element**

Diagram

**Solvers**

- Update Connector Ends - updates the Connector Ends to the correct ones and/or moves the Connector to the correct owner.
- Delete from Diagram - deletes the Connector from the Diagram.
- Delete from Model - deletes the Connector from the Model.

**Example**

###### **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**The diagram owned by the Block *Vehicle In Use*displays a connector between Part Properties typed by *VCCU* and *Cabin*, both of which are owned by the Block *Vehicle In Use*, but the connector is owned by the Block *Vehicle In Maintenance*. 
The error is resolved via the **Update Connector Ends**solver, which moves the Connector under the Block *Vehicle in Use*that owns the diagram and the Part Properties connected by the connector.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>InvConnector</p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if the <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Connector's owner is wrong or if the Connector's ends specified in the model are changed.</span></p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>error</p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Diagram</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><ul><li><strong>Update Connector Ends</strong> - updates the Connector Ends to the correct ones and/or moves the Connector to the correct owner. </li><li><strong>Delete from Diagram </strong>- deletes the Connector from the Diagram.</li><li><strong>Delete from Model </strong>- deletes the Connector from the Model.</li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><strong><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Invalid Connector (Symbols).png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Invalid Connector (Symbols)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong>The diagram owned by the Block <em>Vehicle In Use </em>displays a connector between Part Properties typed by <em>VCCU</em> and <em>Cabin</em>, both of which are owned by the Block <em>Vehicle In Use</em>, but the connector is owned by the Block <em>Vehicle In Maintenance</em>. <br />The error is resolved via the <strong>Update Connector Ends </strong>solver, which moves the Connector under the Block <em>Vehicle in Use </em>that owns the diagram and the Part Properties connected by the connector.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673127 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00643: Invalid Connector (UML correctness constraints)

- page_id: `169673127`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673127/Invalid+Connector+UML+correctness+constraints
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-06-02T12:23:50.093+02:00`
- ancestors: UML correctness constraints (in progress)
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

InvConnector

**Description**

This validation rule checks if the Connector's owner is wrong or if the Connector's ends specified in the model are changed.

**Severity**

error

**Constrained Element**

Connector

**Solvers**

- Delete from Model - deletes the Connector from the Model.
- To fix this, make sure the Connector's owner is correct.

**Example**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### The Block *Vehicle In Maintenance* owns a connector connecting Part Properties (typed by *VCCU*and *Cabin*) owned by another Block*Vehicle In Use*, which causes an error. 
The error is resolved by moving the connector from the Block *Vehicle In Maintenance* to the Block *Vehicle In Use* that owns the Part Properties connected by the connector.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>InvConnector</p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if the <span>Connector's owner is wrong or if the Connector's ends specified in the model are changed.</span></p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>error</p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Connector</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><ul><li><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="de9912e4-bfb7-4b2e-a781-9ce9d7a1ec2e">Delete from Model </ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="de9912e4-bfb7-4b2e-a781-9ce9d7a1ec2e">- deletes the Connector from the Model.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="de9912e4-bfb7-4b2e-a781-9ce9d7a1ec2e">To fix this, make sure the </ac:inline-comment-marker><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="de9912e4-bfb7-4b2e-a781-9ce9d7a1ec2e">Connector's owner is correct.</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p><strong><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Invalid Connector.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Invalid Connector (UML correctness constraints)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Block <em>Vehicle In Maintenance</em> owns a connector connecting Part Properties (typed by <em>VCCU </em>and <em>Cabin</em>) owned by another Block<em> Vehicle In Use</em>, which causes an error. <br />The error is resolved by moving the connector from the Block <em>Vehicle In Maintenance</em> to the Block <em>Vehicle In Use</em> that owns the Part Properties connected by the connector.</h6>
````

In this example, the “<strong>getStereotypeProperty</strong>” method is not a valid method of the<strong> $report</strong> variable.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Invalid Method Reference Warning Message" ac:alt="Invalid Method Reference Warning Message"><ri:attachment ri:filename="invalid_method_reference.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Invalid method reference" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Invalid Method Reference Warning Message.</h6>

## PAGE 00645: Invalid property

- page_id: `169678562`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678562/Invalid+property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-12-24T11:25:31.999+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Debug report template
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The warning, see figure below will open when an invalid property is encountered during the report generation. For example, getting a text property from the **$package** variable, when the text property is not a valid property for **$package**, is a variable with an invalid property that will trigger a warning.

### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Invalid Property Warning Message.

````html
<p>The warning, see figure below will open when an invalid property is encountered during the report generation. For example, getting a text property from the <strong>$package</strong> variable, when the text property is not a valid property for <strong>$package</strong>, is a variable with an invalid property that will trigger a warning.</p><h1 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Invalid Property Warning Message" ac:alt="Invalid Property Warning Message"><ri:attachment ri:filename="invalid_property.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Invalid property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h1><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Invalid Property Warning Message.</h6>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673092 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00646: Invalid Property (UML correctness rules)

- page_id: `169673092`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673092/Invalid+Property+UML+correctness+rules
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-10T12:48:55.156+02:00`
- ancestors: UML correctness rules (in progress)
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

InvProp

**Description**

This validation rule checks if a property is not owned or inherited by the context/type of a nesting element.

**Severity**

error

**Constrained Element**

Diagram

**Solvers**

- Remove from Diagram - removes the property's symbol from the diagram.

**Example**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### The Part Property *human-Machine Interaction*is displayed in a diagram owned by the Block *Vehicle In Use*, but the Part Property is owned by the Block *VCCU*, which causes an error. 
The error is resolved via the **Remove from Diagram**solver, which removes the Part Property *human-Machine Interaction*symbols from the diagram.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>InvProp</p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if a property is not owned or inherited by the context/type of a nesting element.</p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>error</p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Diagram</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><ul><li><strong>Remove from Diagram</strong> - removes the property's symbol from the diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p><strong><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Invalid Property.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Invalid Property (UML correctness rules)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Part Property <em>human-Machine Interaction </em>is displayed in a diagram owned by the Block <em>Vehicle In Use</em>, but the Part Property is owned by the Block <em>VCCU</em>, which causes an error.<br />The error is resolved via the <strong>Remove from Diagram </strong>solver, which removes the Part Property <em>human-Machine Interaction </em>symbols from the diagram.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676990 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00647: Invalid Redefinition

- page_id: `169676990`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676990/Invalid+Redefinition
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-04T17:50:37.363+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Validation > UML validation rules > UML correctness constraints rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

InvRdf****

**Description**

This validation rule checks if a property redefines the inherited property of the owner classifier.

**Severity**

Error

**Constrained Element**

Property, Operation

**Solvers**

To fix this, do one of the following:

- Change the Redefined Property value to the appropriate one that is inherited, not owned.
- Remove Element solver - removes the invalid redefining property from the model.

**Example**

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The element *Car* owns two attributes, *wheel* and *type*. The attribute*wheel*redefines the attribute *type*, causing the validation error. 
The error is resolved by changing the *wheel*attribute's **Redefined Property**value to the appropriate one, which is the *wheel*attribute, inherited from the element *Transport*.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>InvRdf<strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if a property redefines the inherited property of the owner classifier.</p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>Error</p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Property, Operation</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><p>To fix this, do one of the following:</p><ul><li>Change the <strong>Redefined Property </strong>value to the appropriate one that is inherited, not owned.</li><li><strong>Remove Element</strong> solver -  removes the invalid redefining property from the model.</li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="invalid_redefinition.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Invalid Redefinition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">The element <em>Car</em> owns two attributes, <em>wheel</em> and <em>type</em>. The attribute </span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);"><em>wheel</em> </span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">redefines the attribute <em>type</em>, causing the validation error.<br />The error is resolved by changing the <em>wheel </em>attribute's <strong>Redefined Property </strong>value to the appropriate one, which is the <em>wheel </em>attribute, inherited from the element <em>Transport</em></span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">.</span></h6>
````

## PAGE 00652: Java Annotation Member

- page_id: `169679285`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679285/Java+Annotation+Member
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T17:11:29.317+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431974293

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431974296

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431974295

A Java annotation member is mapped directly to the UML Operation owned by the interface stereotyped as «JavaAnnotation». This operation, by itself, can have the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember». However, it is optional, unless specify a default value for it.

A Java annotation member type is mapped to a UML Type property of a UML Parameter with the “Return“ direction kind.

Annotation member mapping table:

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Annotation member declaration | UML Operation with the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember» (Optional), owned by a UML Interface with the stereotype «JavaAnnotation». |
| Annotation member name | UML Operation Name |
| Annotation member documentation | UML Operation Documentation |
| Annotation member type | Mapped to the UML Parameter type with the “return“ direction kind (resides in UML Operation parameters list) |
| Annotation member type modifier | Mapped to the property “Type Modifier“ |
| Default value | {JavaAnnotationMemberDefaultValue} tagged value of a «JavaAnnotationMember» stereotype. The stereotype is set on UML Operation |

#### TIP: Example

Example

Java source code

```java

```

UML model

The stereotype is set on UML Operation</span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6222f7c8-85f8-4edc-9875-549d33a27403"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Java source code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fb1c4bf0-261c-43a5-926c-ad1a3bf3a32e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[/**

## PAGE 00653: Java Annotation Type

- page_id: `169679283`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679283/Java+Annotation+Type
- version_number: 1

- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431959380

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431959383

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431959382

A Java annotation declaration is mapped directly to the UML Interface with the stereotype «JavaAnnotation». Annotation modifiers are mapped into UML Interface properties. Annotation members are mapped to the UML Interface operations with the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember».

Annotation mapping table

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Annotation declaration | UML Interface with stereotype «JavaAnnotation» (optional) |
| Annotation name | UML Interface name |
| Annotation documentation | UML Interface Documentation |
| Visibility modifier | UML Interface "Visibility" property |

#### TIP: Example

Example

Java source code

```java

```

## PAGE 00654: Java Annotations Usage

- page_id: `169679286`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679286/Java+Annotations+Usage

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

431991466

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431991468

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431991467

You can annotate Java elements. This annotation is mapped on top of the {JavaAnnotations} tagged value of the stereotype «JavaElement». «JavaElement» is a base stereotype for all stereotypes used in Java mapping, and can be used directly or with any other stereotype derived from it. Annotation is mapped as a simple string value.
