## PAGE 00655: Java CE Options

- page_id: `169679291`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679291/Java+CE+Options
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-10-09T17:32:41.338+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Java CE options are visible every time you attempt to reverse source code. This section describes the **Resolve collection generic**option for Java-specific options. Other options are common for all languages and are described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reverse Options' space='MD2024xR1'].

##### Resolve collection generics option

By default, this option is selected in the **Reverse Options** dialog. Java introduced parameterized types in JLS 3 and added type variables to all Java collections. With reverse engineering, it is possible to find which type is in a Java collection and make associations directly to the contained type, instead of the Java collection.

Reverse engineering allows you to find a Java parameterized collection and retrieves the Java type used in a container. This type is set as a *Type* to the UML Property. The container type is set to the UML Property/Java language property “Container“ as a simple string.

#### TIP: Example

Example

**Source code sample**

```java
attribute; 
} ]]>
```

```text

```

UML Model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Attribute type, retrieved from collection

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Collection type in UML Property specification

Note, that*$$type$$* shows where it should be in the lined UML Property type on code generation.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Java CE options are visible every time you attempt to reverse source code. This section describes the <strong>Resolve collection generic </strong>option for Java-specific options. Other options are common for all languages and are described in <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Reverse Options" /></ac:link>.</p><h3>Resolve collection generics option</h3><p>By default, this option is selected in the <strong>Reverse Options</strong> dialog. Java introduced parameterized types in JLS 3 and added type variables to all Java collections. With reverse engineering, it is possible to find which type is in a Java collection and make associations directly to the contained type, instead of the Java collection.</p><p>Reverse engineering allows you to find a Java parameterized collection and retrieves the Java type used in a container. This type is set as a <em>Type</em> to the UML Property. The container type is set to the UML Property/Java language property “Container“ as a simple string.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="517c7ceb-087e-49e9-b601-303c1bb3e9f5"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Source code sample</strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bd0fec67-0cd6-494d-8598-ea0278a183c8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[public class Test 
{ 
    java.util.List<String> attribute; 
} ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><pre><br /></pre><p>UML Model</p><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="69"><ri:attachment ri:filename="attribute_type_retrieved_from_collection.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Java CE Options" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">Attribute type, retrieved from collection </span></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="container_property.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Java CE Options" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6 style="text-align: center;">Collection type in UML Property specification</h6><p>Note, that<em> $$type$$</em> shows where it should be in the lined UML Property type on code generation. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679272 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00656: Java Class

- page_id: `169679272`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679272/Java+Class
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T11:10:46.140+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431638635

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431638638

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431638636

A Java class is mapped directly to the UML [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MD2024xR1']with the stereotype «JavaClass». This stereotype is optional and if the UML Class has no stereotype, Java CE treats it as a Java class. If no appropriate property is found in UML,class modifiers are mapped into UML Class properties or to the Java language properties for Class.

Java class fields, operations and inner classes are mapped to the appropriate UML Properties, UML Operations and UML Classes.

**Class mapping table**

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Class declaration | UML Class with stereotype «JavaClass» (optional) |
| Class name | UML Class name |
| Class Documentation | UML Class Documentation |
| Class extends Clause | Mapped to the UML Generalization relationship, where the supplier is an extended class and the client is a mapped class |
| Class Implementation Clause | Mapped to the UML Interface Realization relationship, where the supplier is an extended class and the client is a mapped class. |
| Visibility Modifier | UML Class “Visibility” property |
| Abstract Modifier | UML Class “Is Abstract” property |
| Final Modifier | UML Class “Is Final Specialization“ property |
| Static Modifier | Java Language property “Static modifier“ |
| Strictfp Modifier | Java Language property “Strictfp modifier“ |

#### TIP: Example

Java source code

```java

```

## PAGE 00657: Java Code Engineering

- page_id: `169679263`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679263/Java+Code+Engineering
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T11:50:07.358+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1295392607

1295392609

1295392608

This chapter describes how our modeling tool maps Java language elements to UML, helps choose which profiles to use, and describes Java code engineering properties.

This chapter features the following:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Mapping to UML' space='MD2024xR1'] describes general rules our modeling tool uses to map each Java element to UML and which profile is used. You will find an example and corresponding model with marked properties used in Java to describe mapping rules.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java CE Options' space='MD2024xR1'] introduces specific Java options.
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Method Implementation Reverse' space='MD2024xR1'] describes a functionality that switches Java from a Sequence Diagram to our modeling tool.

##### References

Gosling, J., Joy, B., Steele, G., Bracha, G., & Buckley, A. (2012, February 6). The JavaTM Language Specifica- tion. Java SE 7 Edition. Retrieved March 29, 2012, from [http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf](http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf)

##### Java support in modeling tools

You can perform the following actions:

- Import Java source code into a model (reverse engineering).
- Generate Java code from the model (code generation).
- Apply changes to the source code from the model (round-trip). You can change Java declaration headers and apply them to existing source code; however, you cannot change the method of implementation.
- Create a sequence diagram from the selected method body.
- Create a model from the Java byte code.

1295392606

**Related pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering' space='MD2024xR1']

Retrieved March 29, 2012, from <span style="color: rgb(90,109,148);"><a href="http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf">http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf</a></span></p><h3>Java support in modeling tools</h3><p>You can perform the following actions:</p><ul><li>Import Java source code into a model (reverse engineering).</li><li>Generate Java code from the model (code generation).</li><li>Apply changes to the source code from the model (round-trip). You can change Java declaration headers and apply them to existing source code; however, you cannot change the method of implementation.</li><li>Create a sequence diagram from the selected method body.</li><li>Create a model from the Java byte code. </li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06e9267e-d31b-401b-b0fc-4296dac0206a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1295392606</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong>:</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="e2e6db67-0699-46af-9992-06fee882a68a" /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="f267aa07-7072-427f-8ea2-77a199be81aa"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Code Engineering" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00658: Java Documentation Properties dialog box

- page_id: `169679258`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679258/Java+Documentation+Properties+dialog+box

- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Global options for Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

419370658

419370660

419370659

To open the **Java Documentation Properties**dialog box

1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting project options' space='MD2024xR1']**Project Options** dialog box .
2. In the Options list, expand Code Engineering and click Java Language Options .
3. In the Java Language Options area, select Java Doc as the Processor for Documentation .
4. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='three-dot-btn1.png'] to open the Documentation Properties dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Documentation Properties dialog box

| Box Name | Function |
| --- | --- |
| Tag Name | Type the tag name. |
| Value | Type the value of the tag. |
| Generate | The selected tag will be placed in the generated code as a comment before a classifier (class or interface), operation or attribute. |
| Up | Moves the selected item up the list. |
| Down | Moves the selected item down the list. |
| Add | Adds a new item in the list. |
| Remove | Removes the selected from the list. |
| OK | Saves changes and closes the dialog box. |
| Cancel | Closes the dialog box without saving changes. |
| Help | Displays help. |

419370657

## PAGE 00659: Java Enumeration

- page_id: `169679279`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679279/Java+Enumeration
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-30T13:03:27.209+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431929426

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431929428

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431929427

Java enumeration is mapped directly to the UML Class with the stereotype «JavaEnumeration». If no appropriate property is found in UML, enumeration modifiers are mapped into UML Class properties or to the Java language properties for the interface. Java enumeration literals are mapped to the UML Property with the stereotype «JavaEnumerationLiteral». All contained fields, operations, and inner classes are mapped to appropriate UML Properties, UML Operations, UML Classes. All mapping rules used in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Class' space='MD2024xR1'] mapping apply to the Java enumeration.


## PAGE 00660: Java Enumeration Literal

- page_id: `169679281`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679281/Java+Enumeration+Literal
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:38:35.702+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431945117

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431945119

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431945118

Java Enumeration Literal is mapped directly to the UML Property with the stereotype «JavaEnumerationLiteral». It does not require specific modifiers.

| Java Element | UML Element |
| --- | --- |
| Literal declaration | UML Parameter with stereotype «JavaEnumerationLiteral», owned by UML Class with stereotype «JavaEnumeration» |
| Literal name | UML Property Name |
| Literal documentation | When using JavaDoc preprocessing, it is mapped to the UML Parameter Documentation; otherwise, it is part of the UML Operation Documentation. |

## PAGE 00661: Java Field

- page_id: `169679274`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679274/Java+Field
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T15:08:46.146+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Examples

431872159

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Examples

431872162

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Examples

431872161

A Java field is mapped directly to the UML [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Attribute' space='MD2024xR1'] with the stereotype «JavaField». This stereotype is optional and if a UML Class has no stereotype, Java CE treats it as a Java field. If no appropriate property is found in UML, field modifiers are mapped into the UML Property properties or to the Java language properties.

Note that Java field type modifiers are mapped to the specific property *Type Modifier*, but not to the UML Multiplicity.

**Field mapping table**

| Java Element | UML Element |
| --- | --- |
| Field Declaration | UML Property, owned by UML Class, with stereotype «JavaProperty» (optional) |
| Field Name | UML Property Name |
| Field Documentation | UML Field Documentation |
| Field Type | Mapped to the UML Type property. It is a reference to the UML Classifier; its package structure and name represent the referenced Java class. |
| Field Type Modifiers | Mapped to the specified property Type Modifier |
| Visibility Modifier | UML Property Visibility property |
| Final Modifier | UML Property Is Read Only property |
| Static Modifier | UML Property Is Static property |
| Transient Modifier | Java Language property Transient modifier |
| Volatile Modifier | Java Language property Volatile modifier |

#### TIP: Examples

Examples

Java source code

```java

```

## PAGE 00662: Java Imports

- page_id: `169679290`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679290/Java+Imports
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T14:51:26.123+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

432040070

432040072

432040071

| Java Element | UML Element |
| --- | --- |
| Type Import | Mapped to the UML Element Import with the optional stereotype «JavaImport». The supplier is an imported UML Classifier, representing the imported Java type, and the client is a UML Classifier requiring an imported element. |
| Package Import | Mapped to the UML Package Import with the optional stereotype «JavaImport». The supplier is an imported UML Package, representing the imported Java package, and the client is a UML Classifier requiring imported elements. |
| Static import for all static members | Mapped to the UML Element Import with the stereotype «JavaStaticImport». The {JavaImportAll} tag of «JavaStat- icImport» must have a “true“ value. The supplier is an imported UML Classifier owner of imported static members, and the client is a UML Classifier requiring imported elements. |
| Static import for single static members | Mapped to the UML Element Import with the stereotype «JavaStaticImport». The {JavaImportAll} tag of «JavaStaticImport» must have a “false“ value and its tag {JavaImportedMember} must have reference to the imported member. The supplier is a UML Classifier owner of an imported static member, and the client is a UML Classifier requiring imported elements. If several static members are imported, {JavaImportedMember} can list all of them. |

432040069

## PAGE 00663: Java Interface

- page_id: `169679278`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`

- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

431916479

431916481

431916480

The Java interface is mapped directly to the UML Interface with the stereotype «JavaInterface». This stereotype is optional, and if the UML Interface has no stereotype, Java CE treats it as a Java interface. If no appropriate property is found in the UMLinterface, modifiers are mapped into UML Interface properties or to the Java language properties for the interface. All mapping rules used in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Class' space='MD2024xR1'] mapping apply to the Java interface.

431916478

## PAGE 00664: Java Language Options

- page_id: `169679294`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679294/Java+Language+Options
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T17:13:02.875+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java CE Options
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

432105795

432105798

432105796

You can find these options at the **Options** > **Project** > **Code Engineering** > **Java Language Options**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Java language options

##### Generate Opening Bracket In New Line

By default is turned on. If element (Java type or operation) is generated for the first time into source, curly bracket is generated from the new line, if options is on, or in the same line as declaration header ends, if option is off.

##### Generate Spaces

By default it is turned on. If option is on - adds additional space after open bracket and before close bracket in parameter declaration list.

##### Generate Empty Documentation

By default is turned off. If option is turned on, the modeling tool is generating documentation to the source even, if there are no documentation in model. Just adds Java documentation start and ending symbols.

Here is generated class header with options turned on. UML Class A doesn’t have documentation, but still in code is added documentation elements.

```text
/* *
```

```text
*
```

```text
*/
```

```text
class A
```

##### Automatic “import“ Generation

By default is turned on. If option is turned on, all required imports are added automatically by used references on code generation. If this option is off, than imports are managed by user using specific mapping, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Imports' space='MD2024xR1'].

Note, that if option is turned off, than no imports are created automatically and all imports, retrieved from source code on reverse engineering, are mapped into UML relationships as described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Imports' space='MD2024xR1'].

##### Class Count To Generate Import On Demand

By default value is 10. It means, that if there will be 9 references from the same package to different types, then imports will be generated explicitly to these classes, but if there will be 10 and more references, than it will be generated one import to the package.

This option is valid only, when “Automatic import generation“ is turned on.

##### Documentation

Java documentation has two options by itself. It has **Processor** and **Style**. Style is used to define how to format documentation by adding some comments.

There are predefined two styles:

- Style 1

```text
/* *
```

```text
*
```

```text
*/
```

- Style 2

```text
/* *
```

```text
*
```

```text
*/
```

Processor is responsible for analyzing documentation context. There are two types <none> and Java Doc. <none> options does nothing with documentation and just set it as is on element.

Java Doc is processing documentation by resolving parameter tags or on code generation building documentation by collecting comments from the UML Parameters and adding missed tags for thrown exceptions or return.

There are additional Java Doc options (button “...”). These options can be used to declare what tags would not be generated or what order to use on code generation or perhaps to add some additional tag to documentation for all elements.

Note, that Java Doc processor splits operation documentation for UML Parameter and UML Operation on source code reverse and on code generation UML Parameter documentation is used to build Java documentation for operation.

##### Classpath

You can define classpath here by referencing **jar** files or **class** files directories.

This options is used by Java reverse engineering. If referenced element is not found in model for some reasons, that it is searched in this defined path. And if class is matched by name, this class is added into model.

By default, the modeling tool imports boot classpath of the JVM, on which is running.

##### Java Source

By default is Java 5.0. There are options **1.4** and **5.0**. If you are reversing older specification source code, where, for example “**enum**“ is not a keyword and can be a variable name, then you will need to choose **1.4** Java source, else the modeling tool parser can emit error.

##### Header

It is a header for a newly generated Java files. There can be added some template string which will be preprocessed on writing to source code.

| Template Strings |  |
| --- | --- |
| $FILE_NAME | File name, without a path |
| $DATE | System date |
| $TIME | System time |
| $AUTHOR | User name on the system |

432105794

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java CE Options' space='MD2024xR1']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering' space='MD2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9a75a8f0-2498-4e90-9bb2-437ed50fe7af"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">432105795</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="93bf991b-ea7f-4f01-9aef-ebb7b2cb3239"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">432105798</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="81ed0338-bb30-4079-8b57-ff0d97f2df38"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">432105796</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can find these options at the <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project</strong> &gt; <strong>Code Engineering</strong> &gt; <strong>Java Language Options</strong>.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2018-2-28_11-33-26.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Java Language Options" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></h6><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span>Java language options</span></h6><h3>Generate Opening Bracket In New Line</h3><p>By default is turned on. If element (Java type or operation) is generated for the first time into source, curly bracket is generated from the new line, if options is on, or in the same line as declaration header ends, if option is off.</p><h3>Generate Spaces</h3><p>By default it is turned on. If option is on - adds additional space after open bracket and before close bracket in parameter declaration list.</p><h3>Generate Empty Documentation</h3><p>By default is turned off. If option is turned on, the modeling tool is generating documentation to the source even, if there are no documentation in model. Just adds Java documentation start and ending symbols.</p><p>Here is generated class header with options turned on. UML Class A doesn’t have documentation, but still in code is added documentation elements.</p><pre>/* * </pre><pre> * </pre><pre> */</pre><pre>class A</pre><h3>Automatic “import“ Generation</h3><p>By default is turned on. If option is turned on, all required imports are added automatically by used references on code generation. If this option is off, than imports are managed by user using specific mapping, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Java Imports" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Imports]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>Note, that if option is turned off, than no imports are created automatically and all imports, retrieved from source code on reverse engineering, are mapped into UML relationships as described in <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Java Imports" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Imports]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h3>Class Count To Generate Import On Demand</h3><p>By default value is 10. It means, that if there will be 9 references from the same package to different types, then imports will be generated explicitly to these classes, but if there will be 10 and more references, than it will be generated one import to the package.</p><p>This option is valid only, when “Automatic import generation“ is turned on.</p><h3>Documentation</h3><p>Java documentation has two options by itself. It has <strong>Processor</strong> and <strong>Style</strong>. Style is used to define how to format documentation by adding some comments.</p><p>There are predefined two styles:</p><ul><li>Style 1</li></ul><pre>/* * </pre><pre> *</pre><pre> */</pre><ul><li>Style 2</li></ul><pre> /* * </pre><pre> * </pre><pre> */</pre><p>Processor is responsible for analyzing documentation context. There are two types &lt;none&gt; and Java Doc. &lt;none&gt; options does nothing with documentation and just set it as is on element.</p><p>Java Doc is processing documentation by resolving parameter tags or on code generation building documentation by collecting comments from the UML Parameters and adding missed tags for thrown exceptions or return.</p><p>There are additional Java Doc options (button “...”). These options can be used to declare what tags would not be generated or what order to use on code generation or perhaps to add some additional tag to documentation for all elements.</p><p>Note, that Java Doc processor splits operation documentation for UML Parameter and UML Operation on source code reverse and on code generation UML Parameter documentation is used to build Java documentation for operation.</p><h3>Classpath</h3><p>You can define classpath here by referencing <strong>jar</strong> files or <strong>class</strong> files directories.</p><p>This options is used by Java reverse engineering. If referenced element is not found in model for some reasons, that it is searched in this defined path. And if class is matched by name, this class is added into model.</p><p>By default, the modeling tool imports boot classpath of the JVM, on which is running.</p><h3>Java Source</h3><p>By default is Java 5.0. There are options <strong>1.4</strong> and <strong>5.0</strong>. If you are reversing older specification source code, where, for example “<strong>enum</strong>“ is not a keyword and can be a variable name, then you will need to choose <strong>1.4</strong> Java source, else the modeling tool parser can emit error.</p><h3><span>Header </span></h3><p>It is a header for a newly generated Java files. There can be added some template string which will be preprocessed on writing to source code.</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 40.853657%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 51.870323%;" /><col style="width: 48.129677%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th style="text-align: center;" colspan="2">Template Strings</th></tr><tr><td>$FILE_NAME</td><td>File name, without a path</td></tr><tr><td>$DATE</td><td>System date</td></tr><tr><td>$TIME</td><td>System time</td></tr><tr><td>$AUTHOR</td><td>User name on the system</td></tr></tbody></table></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aec5c50e-2b42-479c-93e8-b40bbfdae4b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">432105794</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="bcdd00b0-7443-40e7-87e0-5df0a2ae412e"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Java CE Options" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="b9ac038b-25f4-4616-930b-ca7bcfce08b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Code Engineering" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679264 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00665: Java Mapping to UML

- page_id: `169679264`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679264/Java+Mapping+to+UML
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:36:14.054+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering
- labels: ['java-profile']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1295645483

1295645486

1295645484

##### Java Profile

Java Profile is used to map the Java language to UML.

UML specification does not provide elements to fully cover JLS; thus, a modeling tool uses UML stereotypes to mark a UML class or interface to be a specific Java element.

Java stereotypes are provided in the *Java_Profile.xml file* in the profiles directory. Some stereotypes have tagged values used for mapping special Java language elements or keywords that are not mapped to standard UML properties. All Java stereotypes are derived from the «JavaElement». Another abstract stereotype, «JavaTypeElement», is used to group all Java type elements. These two stereotypes are abstract and are not used directly.

Each stereotype is used to represent an appropriate Java element:

- «JavaImport» represents [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Imports' space='MD2024xR1'] ,
- «JavaOperation» represents [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Operation' space='MD2024xR1'] , and
- «JavaClass» represents [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Class' space='MD2024xR1'] type.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Stereotypes.

1295645482

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering' space='MD2024xR1']

Some stereotypes have tagged values used for mapping special Java language elements or keywords that are not mapped to standard UML properties. All Java stereotypes are derived from the «JavaElement». Another abstract stereotype, «JavaTypeElement», is used to group all Java type elements. These two stereotypes are abstract and are not used directly.</p><p>Each stereotype is used to represent an appropriate Java element:</p><ul><li>«JavaImport» represents <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Java Imports" /></ac:link>,</li><li>«JavaOperation» represents <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Java Operation" /></ac:link>, and </li><li>«JavaClass» represents <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Java Class" /></ac:link> type.</li></ul><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="stereotype_structure_ce.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Java Mapping to UML" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Stereotypes.</h6><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ebdcffba-83e4-4cfc-8e31-81cb405f9b98"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1295645482</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="cf49da9d-a381-49e7-918b-7c2d824997da" /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="1b9c82d4-5c26-4bdd-a7a4-aa7a57c7a60d"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Code Engineering" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00666: Java Operation

- page_id: `169679276`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679276/Java+Operation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T16:29:22.226+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Examples

431890768

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Examples

431890770

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Examples

431890769

The Java Operation mapped directly to the UML Operation is done with the stereotype «JavaOperation». This stereotype is optional, so if the UML class has no stereotype Java CE treats it as a Java Operation. If no appropriate property is found in UMLOperation, modifiers are mapped into UML Property properties or to the Java language properties. The Java Operation return type is mapped to the UML Type property of the UML Parameter with the “Return“ direction kind. Java Operation parameters are mapped to the UML Parameters. The direction kind is set “In“ for “final” parameters. Note that Java parameter type modifiers are mapped to the UML specific property “Type Modifier,“ but not to the UML Multiplicity.

**Operation mapping table**

| Java Element | UML Element |
| --- | --- |
| Operation declaration | UML Operation, owned by UML Class, with stereotype «JavaOperation» (optional) |
| Operation Name | UML Operation Name |
| Operation documentation | UML Operation Documentation |
| Parameters list | UML Operation “Parameters” list |
| Return type | Mapped to the UML Parameter type with “return“ direction kind, i.e., it resides in the UML Operation parameters list. It is a reference to the UML Classifier; its package structure and name represent the referenced Java class |
| Return type modifiers | Mapped to the modeling tool specified property “Type Modifier“ |
| Visibility modifier | UML Operation “Visibility” property |
| Final modifier | UML Operation “Is Leaf“ property |
| Abstract modifier | UML Operation “Is Abstract“ property |
| Static modifier | UML Operation “Is Static“ property |
| Synchronized modifier | UML Operation “Concurrency“ kind “guarded” |
| Throws list | UML Operation “Raised Exception“ list. It is a list of references to the UML Classes; its package structure and name represent the referenced Java exception class. |
| Native modifier | Java Language property “Native modifier” |
| Strictfp modifier | Java Language property “Strictfp modifier” |

**Parameter mapping table**

| Java Element | UML Element |
| --- | --- |
| Parameter declaration | UML Parameter, owned by UML Operation, with stereotype «JavaParameter» (optional) |
| Parameter name | UML Parameter Name |
| Parameter documentation | When used in JavaDoc preprocessing, it is mapped to the UML Parameter Documentation. If not, it is part of the UML Operation Documentation. |
| Return type | Mapped to the UML Type property. It is a reference to the UML Classifier; its package structure and name represent the referenced Java exception class. |
| Return type modifiers | Mapped to the modeling tool specified property “Type Modifier“ |
| Final modifier | Direction kind “in“ of the UML Parameter |

#### TIP: Examples

```java

```

It is a reference to the UML Classifier<span>; its package structure and name represent the referenced Java exception class.</span> </span></p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Return type modifiers</strong></td><td><p>Mapped to the modeling tool specified property “Type Modifier“ </p></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Final modifier</strong></td><td><p><span>Direction kind “in“ of the UML Parameter</span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="96228ad8-55e4-4aa7-ac65-19becf4c3302"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Examples</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Java Source Code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7e7f32cd-69f5-43ef-bdd8-7f378ec7c929"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[public class MyList

## PAGE 00667: Java Package

- page_id: `169679270`

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

431568590

431568592

431568591

A Java package is mapped to the general UML [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='MD2024xR1']. It does not have any specific [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MD2024xR1']and properties. However, if the UML package represents the Java package, it must not have a «modelLibrary» stereotype from a standard UML profile. The «modelLibrary» stereotype is used to show the root package, where the Java package tree ends and no parent packages, including «modelLibrary», are part of the Java package structure. The figurebelow shows *java.lang.String* added into the *working package*. Packages *java* and *lang* represent general Java packages, but *working package* with *L* is stereotyped with «modelLibrary» and is not part of the Java package.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Package Structure

431568589

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping to UML Rules' space='MD2024xR1']

It does not have any specific <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Stereotype" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[stereotypes ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>and properties. However, if the UML package represents the Java package, it must not have a «modelLibrary» stereotype from a standard UML profile. The «modelLibrary» stereotype is used to show the root package, where the Java package tree ends and no parent packages, including «modelLibrary», are part of the Java package structure. The figure </span>below shows <em>java.lang.String</em> added into the <em>working package</em>. Packages <em>java</em> and <em>lang</em> represent general Java packages, but <em>working package</em> with <em>L</em> is stereotyped with «modelLibrary» and is not part of the Java package.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="package_structure.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Java Package" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">Package Structure</span></h6><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1a3974c8-6575-4cb5-a773-79f783a1bbfa"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431568589</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="edec066a-1303-467e-9645-e3305d60f4b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Mapping to UML Rules" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679289 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00668: Java Parameterized Type

- page_id: `169679289`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679289/Java+Parameterized+Type
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T17:14:36.862+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

432024497

432024500

432024498

Parameterized types are mapped to the general UML Classifier connected with a UML Template Binding to the UML Classifier. The supplier of this binding link is the UML Classifier with UML Template Parameters and represents the Java generic type with type parameters. The client of the UML Template Binding is the UML Classifier of the same UML type as the supplier. Java type parameters are mapped directly to the UML Template Parameter Substitution of the UML Template Binding.

Parameterized type mapping table

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Parametrized type | This is called bounded elements in UML, and is connected to the UML Classifier by the UML Template Binding as a client. The client must be of the same type as the supplier. The supplier must have at least one UML Template Parameter. |
| Parameter for type | Mapped to the UML Template Parameter Substitution of the UML Template Binding. Each UML Template Parameter from the supplier must be substituted by the UML Template Parameter Substitution. The type of the type parameter is any reference to the UML Classifier from the model set as “Actual“ value of the UML Template Parameter Substitution. |
| Type modifiers of the parameter | Mapped to the property “Type modifiers” of the UML Template Parameter Substitution |
| Wildcard | A UML Class with the name “?“ from the Java Profile is used as an “Actual” value in the UML Template Parameter Substitution. |
| Wildcard with bounds | Java bounding type is mapped to the UML Classifier and used as the “Actual” value in UML Template Parameter Substitution. “? extends“ or “? super“ bounding is mapped to the appropriate tag {JavaArgumentBount} value “extends“ or “super“ of the «JavaTypeArgument» stereotype applied to the UML Template Parameter Substitution. |

**Example**

When you have a type with type variables represented in the model, you can create a parameterized type for Java. For this you must create an empty UML Classifier of the same type as the template classifier (type with type variables). Then create a UML Template Binding and create a UML Template Parameter Substitution for the UML Template Parameters.

To create a “java.util.List<String>” type, you must create a UML Interface first, with a UML Template Parameter representing “java.util.List”.

Then create another UML Interface and connect it with the List interface with UML Template Binding.

Note that to draw a UML Template Binding, the client element must have at least one UML Template Parameter created.

You must open the UML Template Binding specification dialog and create a UML Template Parameter Substitution for the appropriate UML Template Parameter.

For a created substitution element, you need to select actual value. In the current example, it is a UML class representing “java.lang.String”.

Now we have created a parameterized type, which can be used in the model to represent type java.util.List<String> parameters.

432024496

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping to UML Rules' space='MD2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e8e66f54-c3b7-4c37-9d33-e0c74c992c99"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">432024497</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae8a9db6-9b48-4a42-be5d-acdca55a52cc"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">432024500</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="db78f954-0fe0-4785-b51b-741c378a6965"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">432024498</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>Parameterized types are mapped to the general UML Classifier connected with a UML Template Binding to the UML Classifier. The supplier of this binding link is the UML Classifier with UML Template Parameters and represents the Java generic type with type parameters. The client of the UML Template Binding is the UML Classifier of the same UML type as the supplier. Java type parameters are mapped directly to the UML Template Parameter Substitution of the UML Template Binding.</span></p><p>Parameterized type mapping table</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Java Element</th><th>UML element</th></tr><tr><td>Parametrized type</td><td><p><span>This is called bounded elements in UML, and is connected to the UML Classifier by the UML Template Binding as a client. The client must be of the same type as the supplier. The supplier must have at least one UML Template Parameter. </span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Parameter for type</td><td><p><span>Mapped to the UML Template Parameter Substitution of the UML Template Binding. Each UML Template Parameter from the supplier must be substituted by the UML Template Parameter Substitution. The type of the type parameter is any reference to the UML Classifier from the model set as “Actual“ value of the UML Template Parameter Substitution.</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Type modifiers of the parameter</td><td><p><span>Mapped to the property “Type modifiers” of the UML Template Parameter Substitution</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Wildcard</td><td><p><span>A UML Class with the name “?“ from the Java Profile is used as an “Actual” value in the UML Template Parameter Substitution.</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Wildcard with bounds</td><td><p><span>Java bounding type is mapped to the UML Classifier and used as the “Actual” value in UML Template Parameter Substitution. “? extends“ or “? super“ bounding is mapped to the appropriate tag {JavaArgumentBount} value “extends“ or “super“ of the «JavaTypeArgument» stereotype applied to the UML Template Parameter Substitution.</span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a30c946b-a6b2-4029-9456-14c16251d33d"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p>When you have a type with type variables represented in the model, you can create a parameterized type for Java. For this you must create an empty UML Classifier of the same type as the template classifier (type with type variables). Then create a UML Template Binding and create a UML Template Parameter Substitution for the UML Template Parameters.</p><p>To create a “java.util.List&lt;String&gt;” type, you must create a UML Interface first, with a UML Template Parameter representing “java.util.List”.</p><p>Then create another UML Interface and connect it with the List interface with UML Template Binding.</p><p>Note that to draw a UML Template Binding, the client element must have at least one UML Template Parameter created.</p><p>You must open the UML Template Binding specification dialog and create a UML Template Parameter Substitution for the appropriate UML Template Parameter. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>For a created substitution element, you need to select actual value. In the current example, it is a UML class representing “java.lang.String”.</span></p><p>Now we have created a parameterized type, which can be used in the model to represent type java.util.List&lt;String&gt; parameters.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="81861f70-4149-491c-b1e4-458a84eb9263"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">432024496</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="cf29fe04-d704-4216-9982-8bb40827f7f0"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Mapping to UML Rules" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00669: Java Referenced Types

- page_id: `169679266`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679266/Java+Referenced+Types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:41:12.294+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431453737

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431453740

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431453739

Java built-in types are used from the UML Standard Profile, which is automatically loaded with every new project.

#### INFO: Show Auxiliary Resources

Show Auxiliary Resources

The UML Standard Profile by default is hidden. If you want to see it, select**Show Auxiliary Resources**in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024xR1'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Every referenced class from the other libraries (including JDK libraries) should be imported/created into a project and referenced in the CES reference path (by default the reference path is *Model* package in the model).

A modeling tool resolves referenced classes from the specified class path (by default, the class path is the boot class path taken from the JVM on which the modeling tool is started). It creates an appropriate package structure as described below in **Sorting reversed classes according to the classpath**.

If a referenced class is not found in the model or the class path, the reference is created in the *Default* package.

Java Profile defines the UML Class with the name "?". It is used for mapping parameterized types.

**Sorting reversed classes according to the classpath**

References are sorted to packages according to the classpath entry.

The figure below shows how the reverse result of the following java code is represented:

#### TIP: Example

java

Example

Java code

```java

```

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

## PAGE 00670: Java Type Variables

- page_id: `169679288`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679288/Java+Type+Variables
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T12:11:55.024+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

432009432

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

432009435

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

432009433

Type variables are mapped to the UML Template Parameter of a UML Class, Interface or UML Operation, according to which Java element has type variables. If bound types are present, they are mapped into the UML Class or UML Interface connected with the UML Generalization or UML Interface Realization as a supplier. The client is a UML Class, which is a “Parametered Element” of the UML Template Parameter.

Type variable mapping table

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Type variable declaration | Mapped to the UML Template Parameter. This UML Template Parameter is of the Class type from the UML Metamodel. The UML Template Parameter has the property “Parametered Element“ of the UML Template Parameter. The metamodel type “Class“ is taken from the “UML Standard profile/UML 2 Metamodel” |
| Type variable name | Mapped to the UML Class name. This UML Class is “Parametered Element“ |
| Type bounds | If a bound type is a Java class, it is mapped to the General class of the “Parametered Element“. If a bound type is a Java interface, than it is mapped to the Realized Interface. |

#### TIP: Example

Example

Java source code****

```java
{ 
    E attribute; 
}]]>
```

###### UML Class with template parameter E, representing Java type variable

In this example, the Template Parameter “E” is a subtype of the Cloneable class (e.g. “java.util.Cloneable“). “E” is used to type the “Test” public class attribute.

###### Template parameters in UML Class specification dialog

432009431

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping to UML Rules' space='MD2024xR1']

If bound types are present, they are mapped into the UML Class or UML Interface connected with the UML Generalization or UML Interface Realization as a supplier. The client is a UML Class, which is a “Parametered Element” of the UML Template Parameter. </span></p><p><br /></p><p>Type variable mapping table</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Java Element</th><th>UML element</th></tr><tr><td>Type variable declaration</td><td>Mapped to the UML Template Parameter. This UML Template Parameter is of the Class type from the UML Metamodel. The UML Template Parameter has the property “Parametered Element“ of the UML Template Parameter. The metamodel type “Class“ is taken from the “UML Standard profile/UML 2 Metamodel”</td></tr><tr><td>Type variable name</td><td>Mapped to the UML Class name. This UML Class is “Parametered Element“</td></tr><tr><td>Type bounds</td><td>If a bound type is a Java class, it is mapped to the General class of the “Parametered Element“. If a bound type is a Java interface, than it is mapped to the Realized Interface. </td></tr></tbody></table><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="539f5b79-31ca-4897-96d3-50445a203caa"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Java source code<strong> </strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="05fc2a6f-84d9-4c23-9732-864e27d56948"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[public class Test <E extends Cloneable> 
````html
{ 
    E attribute; 
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span>UML Class with template parameter E, representing Java type variable</span></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5a801ed3-2475-43be-b637-b529d9dff7b7"><ac:rich-text-body><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span><span>In this example, the  Template Parameter “E” is a subtype of the Cloneable class (e.g. “java.util.Cloneable“). “E” is used to type the “Test”  public class attribute.</span></span></span></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;">Template parameters in UML Class specification dialog </h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e684723f-4982-4b98-ad50-c503d699f2f5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">432009431</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="5912d3be-48bf-4658-bc67-78bad3b0dc1f"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Mapping to UML Rules" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678595 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00671: Javadoc syntax

- page_id: `169678595`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678595/Javadoc+syntax
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-12-24T12:05:22.052+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Javadoc syntax tool
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A doc comment consists of characters between the characters /** that begin the comment and the characters */ that end it. Leading asterisks are allowed on each line and are described below. Text in a comment can continue to multiple lines.

- Leading asterisks When Javadoc Tool parses a doc comment, leading asterisk (*) characters on each line are discarded.
- First sentence The first sentence of each doc comment is a summary sentence. This sentence can be retrieved from $doc.firstSentenceTags. The first sentence tags are a collection of inline tags until full stops (.).
- Comment and tag sections A comment is the main description which begins after the starting delimiters /** and continues until the tag section. A tag section starts with the first block tag. The comment can be retrieved by $doc.comment
- Comments written in HTML Text is written in HTML and will be rendered as HTML support before being printed to a report. (See Appendix D: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Appendix D: HTML tag support' space='MD2024xR1'] )
- **Block and in-line tags** 
A tag is a special keyword within a doc comment that Javadoc Tool can process. There are two kinds of tags: 
**(i) Block tags** 
 They can be placed only in the tag section. The block tag form is @tag. 
 The block tag can be accessed directly from the root document such as $doc.param and $doc.author. 
 The $doc.tags will provide a collection of all the tags appearing in this Javadoc. **(ii) Inline tags** 
 They can be placed anywhere in the main description or in the block tags. The inline tag form is {@tag}.

The table below lists Current Supported Tags

| Tag | JDK | Report Wizard Support |
| --- | --- | --- |
| @author | 1.0 | Yes |
| {@code} | 1.5 | Render as <code>text</code> |
| {@docRoot} | 1.3 | Not supported |
| @deprecated | 1.0 | Yes |
| @exception | 1.0 | Yes |
| {@inheritDoc} | 1.4 | Not supported |
| {@link} | 1.2 | Yes, with conditions (External link, model support, and class/method link will be ignored) |
| {@linkplain} | 1.4 | Yes, see {@link link} |
| {@literal} | 1.5 | Yes |
| @param | 1.0 | Yes |
| @return | 1.0 | Yes |
| @see | 1.0 | Yes, with conditions (External link, model support, and class/method link will be return as plain text) |
| @serial | 1.2 | Yes |
| @serialData | 1.2 | Yes |
| @serialField | 1.2 | Yes |
| @since | 1.1 | Yes |
| @throws | 1.2 | Yes |
| {@value} | 1.4 | Not Supported |
| @version | 1.0 | Yes |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A doc comment consists of characters between the characters /** that begin the comment and the characters */ that end it. Leading asterisks are allowed on each line and are described below. Text in a comment can continue to multiple lines.</p><ul><li><strong>Leading asterisks</strong><br />When Javadoc Tool parses a doc comment, leading asterisk (*) characters on each line are discarded.</li><li><strong>First sentence</strong><br />The first sentence of each doc comment is a summary sentence. <br />This sentence can be retrieved from $doc.firstSentenceTags. <br />The first sentence tags are a collection of inline tags until full stops (.).</li><li><strong>Comment and tag sections</strong><br />A comment is the main description which begins after the starting delimiters /** and <br />continues until the tag section. A tag section starts with the first block tag. The  comment can be retrieved by $doc.comment </li><li><strong>Comments written in HTML</strong><br />Text is written in HTML and will be rendered as HTML support before being printed to a report. (See Appendix D: <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Appendix D: HTML tag support" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[HTML Tag Support]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>)</li><li><p><strong>Block and in-line tags</strong><br />A tag is a special keyword within a doc comment that Javadoc Tool can process. There are two kinds of tags:<br /><strong>     (i) Block tags</strong><br />         They can be placed only in the tag section. The block tag form is @tag. <br />         The block tag can be accessed directly from the root document such as $doc.param and        $doc.author. <br />         The $doc.tags will provide a collection of all the tags appearing in this Javadoc.</p><p> <strong>    (ii) Inline tags</strong><br />          They can be placed anywhere in the main description or in the block tags. The inline tag form is {@tag}.</p></li></ul><p>The table below lists Current Supported Tags</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 38.2806%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 20.8092%;" /><col style="width: 9.44123%;" /><col style="width: 69.8683%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Tag</th><th>JDK</th><th>Report Wizard Support</th></tr><tr><td>@author </td><td>1.0</td><td>Yes</td></tr><tr><td>{@code}</td><td>1.5</td><td>Render as <br />&lt;code&gt;text&lt;/code&gt;</td></tr><tr><td>{@docRoot}</td><td>1.3</td><td>Not supported</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@deprecated</td><td colspan="1">1.0</td><td colspan="1">Yes</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@exception</td><td colspan="1">1.0</td><td colspan="1">Yes</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">{@inheritDoc}</td><td colspan="1">1.4</td><td colspan="1">Not supported</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">{@link}</td><td colspan="1">1.2</td><td colspan="1">Yes, with conditions <br />(External link, model support, <br />and class/method link will be <br />ignored)</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">{@linkplain}</td><td colspan="1">1.4</td><td colspan="1">Yes, see <br />{@link link}</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">{@literal}</td><td colspan="1">1.5</td><td colspan="1">Yes</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@param</td><td colspan="1">1.0</td><td colspan="1">Yes</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@return</td><td colspan="1">1.0</td><td colspan="1">Yes</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@see</td><td colspan="1">1.0</td><td colspan="1">Yes, with conditions <br />(External link, model <br />support, and class/method <br />link will be return as plain text)</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@serial</td><td colspan="1">1.2</td><td colspan="1">Yes</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@serialData</td><td colspan="1">1.2</td><td colspan="1"><span>Yes</span></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@serialField</td><td colspan="1">1.2</td><td colspan="1"><span>Yes</span></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@since</td><td colspan="1">1.1</td><td colspan="1"><span>Yes</span></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@throws</td><td colspan="1">1.2</td><td colspan="1"><span>Yes</span></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">{@value}</td><td colspan="1">1.4</td><td colspan="1">Not Supported</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">@version</td><td colspan="1">1.0</td><td colspan="1"><span>Yes</span></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

## PAGE 00672: Javadoc syntax tool

- page_id: `169678593`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678593/Javadoc+syntax+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-12-24T12:00:33.003+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1338491418

1338491421

1338491421

Javadoc is the industry standard for documenting Java classes. Javadoc can be documented inside the documentation field, see figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Documentation Field.

Report Wizard provides a template tool for translating Javadoc text into Javadoc properties. Javadoc tool will be loaded and used inside the template upon the user’s request.

An example of Javadoc text is shown below:

```text
* Observable. The observer class such as
 * {@link AbstractTemplateEngine} or graphical user
 * interface can catch notified object and interact with
 * user.
 *
 * @author Siri Chongasamethaworn (siri_c@nomagicasia.com)
 * @since Aug 3, 2007
 */]]>
```

To print the Javadoc comment and author inside the template:

```text

```

The output from this example will be:

```text

```

The Javadoc Syntax tool is implemented based on Custom tool (See *[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Appendix A: report extensions' space='ReportWizard183']*).

1338491417

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="42225b76-4a40-4672-bfbb-a9b6d7de725f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1338491418</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="59676b66-86d5-4a64-a3f4-392aac3f8d76"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1338491421</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7e72b6dc-b3a8-4cf6-a2ea-7024c77fbdab"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1338491421</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Javadoc is the industry standard for documenting Java classes. Javadoc can be documented inside the documentation field, see figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Documentation Field" ac:alt="Documentation Field"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Documentation Field.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Javadoc syntax tool" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Documentation Field.</h6><p>Report Wizard provides a template tool for translating Javadoc text into Javadoc properties. Javadoc tool will be loaded and used inside the template upon the user’s request.</p><p>An example of Javadoc text is shown below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8154414e-86db-4ea7-b2f1-7de71b435528"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[/**
 * A base class realizing from interface {@link Itool}.
 * This class provides default implementation for
 * {@link Itool}. The extended class from this class may
 * send message or object to observer class by invoking
 * {@link #notifyObservers(Object)} of class <code>
 * Observable</code>. The observer class such as
 * {@link AbstractTemplateEngine} or graphical user
 * interface can catch notified object and interact with
 * user.
 *
 * @author Siri Chongasamethaworn (siri_c@nomagicasia.com)
 * @since Aug 3, 2007
 */]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To print the Javadoc comment and author inside the template:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="566fb076-8c75-47ad-856e-c1c6acdba6f3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#import ("javadoc", "com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.doc.JavaDoctool")
#set ($doc = $javadoc.create($class.documentation))
Comment:
$doc.comment
Author:
$doc.author]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output from this example will be:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bffdd3df-7bb6-48da-92dc-13497c2b1103"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Comment:
A base class realizing from interface Itool. This class provides default implementation for Itool. The extended class from this class may send message or object to observer class by invoke #notifyObservers(Object) of class Observable. The observer class such as AbstractTemplateEngine or graphical user interface can catch notified object and interact with user.
Author:
Siri Chongasamethaworn (siri_c@nomagicasia.com)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The Javadoc Syntax tool is implemented based on Custom tool (See <em><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="ReportWizard183" ri:content-title="Appendix A: report extensions" /></ac:link></em>).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="86d78850-ded0-402b-b48b-03a13f442ed6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1338491417</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="c8432db8-2e09-41cc-9425-70dc4b6ee233" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678596 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00673: Javadoc tool API

- page_id: `169678596`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678596/Javadoc+tool+API
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-12-24T12:09:05.950+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Javadoc syntax tool
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

133364031

133364033

133364028

**On this page**

43

133364032

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Javadoc Tool API.

#### JavaDocTool

JavaDoc Tool is a custom tool class used for creating a Document node.

- create() - Return a new instance of a Document node. Each JavaDoc comment must start with /** and ended with */.
- createForText() - Return a new instance of a Document node. This method allows in-complete JavaDoc (comments without /** and */) to be loaded with JavaDocTool.

##### Document

Document is an interface representing a Comment Document.

- getRawComment() - Return the full unprocessed text of the comment.
- getTags() - Return all tags in this document item.
- getTags(tagName : String) - Return a tag of the specified kind of this document item.
- get(tagName : String) - Return a tag of the specified kind of this document item. If any tags with the same kind are found, the first tag of that kind will be returned.

##### DocumentImpl

DocumentImpl is a default JavaDoc document implementation.

- getComment() - Return formatted text for this document.
- getInlineTags() - Return comment as a collection of tags.
- getFirstSentenceTags() - Return the first sentence of the comment as a collection of tags.

##### Tag

Tag is an interface representing a simple documentation tag, such as @since, @author, and @version

- getKind() - Return the kind of this tag.
- getText() - Return the text of this tag.
- getInlineTags() - Return a collection of tags for a documentation comment with embedded {@link} tags.

##### TagImpl

TagImpl is a default tag implementation.

- getRawText() - Return the full unprocessed text of this tag.
- toString() - Return the text of this tag. Usually calls getText().

##### ParamTag

ParamTag represents a @param documentation tag.

- getName() - Return the name of the parameter associated with this tag.
- getComment() - Return the parameter’s comment.

##### ThrowsTag

ThrowsTag represents a @throws or @exception documentation tag.

- getName() - Return the name of the exception associated with this tag.
- getComment() - Return the exception’s comment.

##### SeeTag

SeeTag represents a user-defined cross-reference to related documentation. The reference can be either inline with the comment using {@link} or a separate block comment using @see.

- getLabel() - Return the label of the @see tag.
- getReference() - Return the MODEL or URL of the @see reference.
- getLink() - Return the Link object representing this tag.

##### SerialFieldTag

SerialFieldTag represents a @serialField tag.

- getFieldName() - return the serial field name.
- getFieldDescription() - return the field comment.
- getFieldType() - return the field type string.

For examples:

- Import the Javadoc tool and create an instance of Javadoc document:

```text

```

- Print a Javadoc comment:

```text

```

- Print the first encountered author:

```text

```

- Print all authors:

```text

```

- Print the first sentence:

```text

```

- Print the inline tags:

```text

```

- Print a raw comment (plain text without a document parser or HTML renderer):

```text

```

- Print all block tags:

```text

```

- Print all param tags:

```text

```

- Print all throws tags:

```text

```

- Print all see tags:

```text

```

**Reference Documents**

- Javadoc specification [http://java.sun.com/j2se/1.5.0/docs/tooldocs/windows/javadoc.html#javadoctags](http://java.sun.com/j2se/1.5.0/docs/tooldocs/windows/javadoc.html#javadoctags)
- Writing Javadoc [http://java.sun.com/j2se/javadoc/writingdoccomments/index.html](http://java.sun.com/j2se/javadoc/writingdoccomments/index.html)
- Javadoc Tool API <install.dir>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.reportwizard/api/javadoc.zip
