Conversion from the type of the initialization expression to the type of the static const data-member must not require a user-defined conversion. </p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 100.0%;"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>ref class MyClassWithLiteral</pre><pre>{ </pre><pre>literal int i = 1; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p><span>MyClassWithLiteral with «C++CLIClass» </span>Attribute i has «C++CLIAttribute» applied with tagged value field = literal and set to Is Static = true and Is Read Only = true </p></td></tr></tbody></table><h3><span>Inheritance Keywords</span></h3><p>__single_inheritance, __multiple_inheritance, __virtual_inheritance</p><p>Grammar:</p><p>class [__single_inheritance] class-name;</p><p>class [__multiple_inheritance] class-name;</p><p>class [__virtual_inheritance] class-name;</p><p><strong>Parameter</strong></p><p>class-name</p><p>The name of the class being declared. </p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 80.821915%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 49.964622%;" /><col style="width: 50.035378%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>class __single_inheritance S;</pre></td><td><p><span>Class with «VC++Class» </span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><h3><span>Microsoft-Specific Native declaration keywords </span></h3><p><span>__interface</span></p><hr /><p>Grammar:</p><p>modifier __interface interface-name {interface-definition};</p><p>A Visual C++ interface can be defined as follows:</p><ul><li>Can inherit from zero or more base interfaces.</li><li>Cannot inherit from a base class.</li><li>Can only contain public, pure virtual methods.</li><li>Cannot contain constructors, destructors, or operators.</li><li>Cannot contain static methods.</li><li>Cannot contain data members; properties are allowed.</li></ul><table class="relative-table" style="width: 57.83866%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 67.61214%;" /><col style="width: 32.387863%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface MyInterface {}; </pre></td><td><p><br /></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span>__delegate</span></p><hr /><p>Grammar:</p><p>__delegate function-declarator</p><p>A delegate is roughly equivalent to a C++ function pointer except for the following difference:</p><ul><li>A delegate can only be bound to one or more methods within a __gc class.</li></ul><p>When the compiler encounters the __delegate keyword, a definition of a __gc class is generated. This __gc class has the following characteristics:</p><ul><li>It inherits from System::MulticastDelegate.</li><li>It has a constructor that takes two arguments: a pointer to a __gc class or NULL (in the case of binding to a static method) and a fully qualified method of the specified type.</li><li>It has a method called Invoke, whose signature matches the declared signature of the delegate. </li></ul><table class="relative-table" style="width: 57.83866%;"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__delegate int MyDelegate(); </pre></td><td><br /></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span>__event</span></p><hr /><p>Grammar:</p><ul><li><span>__event </span><span>method-declarator</span><span>;</span></li><li><span>__event __interface </span><span>interface-specifier</span><span>; </span></li><li><span>__event </span><span>member-declarator</span><span>;</span></li></ul><h3>Native Events</h3><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>class Source { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event void MyEvent(int i); </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><br /></td></tr></tbody></table><h3>Com Events</h3><p>The __interface keyword is always required after __event for a COM event source.</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>NOTE </strong><span>This mapping produces the exceptional case for syntax checker. The syntax checker has to allow attribute without name for this case. </span></p><h3><span>Managed Events</span></h3><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p>public __delegate void D();</p><p>public __gc class X { public:</p><p><span>__event D* E; </span></p><p><span>__event void noE(); }; </span></p><p><span>NOTE </span><span>__delegate is in C++ Managed Profile whereas __event is in Microsoft Visual C++ Profile. </span></p><h3><span>Microsoft-Specific Modifiers </span></h3><p><strong>Based addressing with __based</strong></p><p>The <strong>__based</strong> keyword allows you to declare pointers based on pointers (pointers that are offsets from existing pointers).</p><p>type <strong>__based</strong>( base ) declarator</p><p>Example</p><p><span>// based_pointers1.cpp </span></p><p><span>// compile with: /c</span></p><p><span>void *vpBuffer;</span></p><p><span>struct llist_t {</span></p><p>    void __based( vpBuffer ) *vpData;</p><p>    struct llist_t __based( vpBuffer ) *llNext;</p><p>};</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>void *vpBuffer;</pre><pre>void __based(vpBuffer) *vpData;</pre></td><td><p><br /></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p>Function calling conventions</p><hr /><p><br /></p><p>The Visual C/C++ compiler provides several different conventions for calling internal and external functions.</p><p><strong>__cdecl</strong></p><p>return-type <strong>__cdecl</strong> function-name[(argument-list)]</p><p>This is the default calling convention for C and C++ programs. Because the stack is cleaned up by the caller, it can do <strong>vararg</strong> functions. The <strong>__cdecl</strong> calling convention creates larger executables than <strong>__stdcall</strong>, because it requires each function call to include stack cleanup code. The following list shows the implementation of this calling convention.</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>class CMyClass {</pre><pre>void __cdecl myMethod(); </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><strong>__clrcall</strong></p><p>return-type <strong>__clrcall</strong> function-name[(argument-list)]</p><p><span>Specifies that a function can only be called from managed code. Use </span><strong>__clrcall </strong><span>for all virtual functions that will only be called from managed code. However, this calling convention cannot be used for functions that will be called from native code. </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>Example </span></p><p><span>// compile with: /clr:oldSyntax /LD </span></p><p><span>void __clrcall Test1( ) {}</span></p><p><span>void (__clrcall *fpTest1)( ) = &amp;Test1; </span></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span><br /></span></p><p><span>Code </span></p><p><span>void __clrcall Test1( ) {} void (__clrcall *fpTest1)( ) = &amp;Test1; </span></p><p><span>MD-UML </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>217 </span></p><p><span>Copyright © 1998-2015 No Magic, Inc. </span></p><p><span>C++ CODE ENGINEERING </span><span>Microsoft C++ Profiles </span></p><p><span>__stdcall </span></p><p><span>return-type </span><span>__stdcall </span><span>function-name[(argument-list)] </span></p><p><span>The </span><span>__stdcall </span><span>calling convention is used to call Win32 API functions. The callee cleans the stack, so the com- piler makes </span><span>vararg </span><span>functions </span><span>__cdecl</span><span>. Functions that use this calling convention require a function prototype. </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>218 Copyright © 1998-2015 No Magic, Inc. </span></p><p><span>C++ CODE ENGINEERING </span><span>Microsoft C++ Profiles </span></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span><br /></span></p><p><span>Code MD-UML </span></p><p><span>class CmyClass2 {<br /> void __stdcall mymethod(); </span></p><p><span>}; </span></p><p><span>__fastcall<br /> </span><span>return-type </span><span>__fastcall </span><span>function-name[(argument-list)] </span></p><p><span>The </span><span>__fastcall </span><span>calling convention specifies that arguments to functions are to be passed in registers, when possible. The following list shows the implementation of this calling convention. </span></p><p><span>Example </span></p><p><span>class CmyClass3 {<br /> void __fastcall mymethod(); </span></p><p><span>}; </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>219 </span></p><p><span>Copyright © 1998-2015 No Magic, Inc. </span></p><p><span>C++ CODE ENGINEERING </span><span>Microsoft C++ Profiles </span></p><table><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span><br /></span></p><p><span>Code MD-UML </span></p><p><span>class CmyClass3 {<br /> void __fastcall mymethod(); </span></p><p><span>}; </span></p><p><span>__thiscall<br /> </span><span>return-type </span><span>__thiscall </span><span>function-name[(argument-list)] </span></p><p><span>The </span><span>__thiscall </span><span>calling convention is used on member functions and is the default calling convention used by C++ member functions that do not use variable arguments. Under </span><span>__thiscall</span><span>, the callee cleans the stack, which is impossible for </span><span>vararg </span><span>functions. Arguments are pushed on the stack from right to left, with the </span><span>this </span><span>pointer being passed via register ECX, and not on the stack, on the x86 architecture. </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>Example </span></p><p><span>// compile with: /c /clr:oldSyntax class CmyClass4 { </span></p><p><span>void __thiscall mymethod(); </span></p><p><span>void __clrcall mymethod2(); }; </span></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span><br /></span></p><p><span>Code </span></p><p><span>class CmyClass4 {<br /> void __thiscall mymethod(); </span></p><p><span>}; </span></p><p><span>MD-UML </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>220 </span></p><p><span>Copyright © 1998-2015 No Magic, Inc. </span></p><p><span>C++ CODE ENGINEERING </span><span>Microsoft C++ Profiles </span></p><p><span>__unaligned<br /> </span><span>type </span><span>__unaligned </span><span>pointer_identifier </span></p><p><span>When a pointer is declared as </span><span>__unaligned</span><span>, the compiler assumes that the type or data pointed to is not aligned. </span><span>__unaligned </span><span>is only valid in compilers for x64 and the Itanium Processor Family (IPF). </span></p><p><span>Example </span></p><p><span>// compile with: /c // processor: x64 IPF #include &lt;stdio.h&gt; int main() { </span></p><p><span>char buf[100];<br /> int __unaligned *p1 = (int*)(&amp;buf[37]); int *p2 = (int *)p1;<br /> *p1=0; //ok<br /> __try { </span></p><p><span>*p2 = 0; // throws an exception } </span></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span>Code </span></p><p><span>__except(1) { puts(&quot;exception&quot;); </span></p><p><span>}} </span></p><p><span>int __unaligned *p1; </span></p><p><span>MD-UML </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>221 </span></p><p><span>Copyright © 1998-2015 No Magic, Inc. </span></p><p><span>C++ CODE ENGINEERING </span><span>Microsoft C++ Profiles </span></p><p><span>__w64 </span></p><p><span>type </span><span>__w64 </span><span>identifier </span></p><p><span>Parameters </span></p><p><span>type </span></p><p><span>One of the three types that might cause problems in code being ported from a 32-bit to a 64-bit compiler: </span><span>int</span><span>, </span><span>long</span><span>, or a pointer. </span></p><p><span>identifier<br /> The identifier for the variable you are creating. </span></p><p><span>__w64 lets you mark variables, such that when you compile with /Wp64 the compiler will report any warnings that would be reported if you were compiling with a 64-bit compiler. </span></p><p><span>Example </span></p><p><span>// compile with: /W3 /Wp64 typedef int Int_32;<br /> #ifdef _WIN64<br /> typedef __int64 Int_Native; #else </span></p><p><span>typedef int __w64 Int_Native; #endif<br /> int main() { </span></p><p><span>Int_32 i0 = 5;<br /> Int_Native i1 = 10;<br /> i0 = i1; // C4244 64-bit int assigned to 32-bit int </span></p><p><span>// char __w64 c; error, cannot use __w64 on char } </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>222 </span></p><p><span>Copyright © 1998-2015 No Magic, Inc. </span></p><p><span>C++ CODE ENGINEERING </span><span>Microsoft C++ Profiles </span></p><table><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span><br /></span></p><p><span>Code </span></p><p><span>int __w64 i; </span></p><p><span>Extended storage-class attributes with __declspec </span></p><p><span>The extended attribute syntax for specifying storage-class information uses the </span><span>__declspec </span><span>keyword, which specifies that an instance of a given type is to be stored with a Microsoft-specific storage-class attribute listed below. Examples of other storage-class modifiers include the </span><span>static </span><span>and </span><span>extern </span><span>keywords. However, these key- words are part of the ANSI specification of the C and C++ languages, and as such are not covered by extended attribute syntax. The extended attribute syntax simplifies and standardizes Microsoft-specific extensions to the C and C++ languages. </span></p><p><span>decl-specifier:<br /> </span><span>__declspec ( </span><span>extended-decl-modifier-seq </span><span>) </span><span>extended-decl-modifier-seq: extended-decl-modifieropt<br /> extended-decl-modifier extended-decl-modifier-seq extended-decl-modifier:<br /> </span><span>align(</span><span>#</span><span>)<br /> allocate(&quot;</span><span>segname</span><span>&quot;)<br /> appdomain<br /> deprecated<br /> dllimport<br /> dllexport<br /> jitintrinsic<br /> naked </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>MD-UML </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>223 Copyright © 1998-2015 No Magic, Inc. </span></p><p><span>C++ CODE ENGINEERING </span><span>Microsoft C++ Profiles </span></p><p><span>noalias<br /> noinline<br /> noreturn<br /> nothrow<br /> novtable<br /> process property({get</span><span>=get_func_name|</span><span>,put</span><span>=put_func_name</span><span>}) restrict </span></p><p><span>selectany<br /> thread uuid(&quot;</span><span>ComObjectGUID</span><span>&quot;) </span></p><p><span>White space separates the declaration modifier sequence. </span></p><p><span>Extended attribute grammar supports these Microsoft-specific storage-class attributes: </span><span>align</span><span>, </span><span>allocate</span><span>, </span><span>appdo- main</span><span>, </span><span>deprecated</span><span>, </span><span>dllexport</span><span>, </span><span>dllimport</span><span>, </span><span>jitintrinsic</span><span>, </span><span>naked</span><span>, </span><span>noalias</span><span>, </span><span>noinline</span><span>, </span><span>noreturn</span><span>, </span><span>nothrow</span><span>, </span><span>novtable</span><span>, </span><span>process</span><span>, </span><span>restrict</span><span>, </span><span>selectany</span><span>, and </span><span>thread</span><span>. It also supports these COM-object attributes: </span><span>property </span><span>and </span><span>uuid</span><span>. </span></p><p><span>The </span><span>dllexport</span><span>, </span><span>dllimport</span><span>, </span><span>naked</span><span>, </span><span>noalias</span><span>, </span><span>nothrow</span><span>, </span><span>property</span><span>, </span><span>restrict</span><span>, </span><span>selectany</span><span>, </span><span>thread</span><span>, and </span><span>uuid </span><span>storage- class attributes are properties only of the declaration of the object or function to which they are applied. The </span><span>thread </span><span>attribute affects data and objects only. The </span><span>naked </span><span>attribute affects functions only. The </span><span>dllimport </span><span>and </span><span>dll- export </span><span>attributes affect functions, data, and objects. The </span><span>property</span><span>, </span><span>selectany</span><span>, and </span><span>uuid </span><span>attributes affect COM objects. </span></p><p><span>The </span><span>__declspec </span><span>keywords should be placed at the beginning of a simple declaration. The compiler ignores, without warning, any </span><span>__declspec </span><span>keywords placed after * or &amp; and in front of the variable identifier in a declara- tion. </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>Note: </span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(90,109,148);">• </span><span>A </span><span>__declspec </span><span>attribute specified in the beginning of a user-defined type declaration applies to the variable of that type. </span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(90,109,148);">• </span><span>A </span><span>__declspec </span><span>attribute placed after the </span><span>class </span><span>or </span><span>struct </span><span>keyword applies to the user-defined type. </span></p><p><br /></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span><br /></span></p><p><span>Code MD-UML </span></p><p><span>class __declspec(dllimport) X {}; </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>224 </span></p><p><span>Copyright © 1998-2015 No Magic, Inc. </span></p><p><span>C++ CODE ENGINEERING </span><span>Microsoft C++ Profiles </span></p><table><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span><br /></span></p><p><span>Code MD-UML </span></p><p><span>__declspec(dllimport) class X {} varX; </span></p><p><span>__restrict </span></p><p><span>The </span><span>__restrict </span><span>keyword is valid only on variables, and </span><span>__declspec(restrict) </span><span>is only valid on function declara- tions and definitions. </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>225 Copyright © 1998-2015 No Magic, Inc. </span></p><p><span>C++ CODE ENGINEERING </span><span>Microsoft C++ Profiles </span></p><p><span>When </span><span>__restrict </span><span>is used, the compiler will not propagate the no-alias property of a variable. That is, if you assign a </span><span>__restrict </span><span>variable to a non-</span><span>__restrict </span><span>variable, the compiler will not imply that the non-</span><span>__restrict </span><span>variable is not aliased. </span></p><p><span>Generally, if you affect the behavior of an entire function, it is better to use the </span><span>__declspec </span><span>than the keyword. </span><span>__restrict </span><span>is similar to </span><span>restrict </span><span>from the C99 spec, but </span><span>__restrict </span><span>can be used in C++ or C programs. </span></p><p><span>Example </span></p><p><span>// __restrict_keyword.c<br /> // compile with: /LD<br /> // In the following function, declare a and b as disjoint arrays // but do not have same assurance for c and d.<br /> void sum2(int n, int * __restrict a, int * __restrict b, </span></p><p><span>int * c, int * d) { int i; </span></p><p><span>for (i = 0; i &lt; n; i++) { a[i] = b[i] + c[i]; c[i] = b[i] + d[i]; </span></p><p><span>} } </span></p><ul><li><p><span>//  By marking union members as __restrict, tells the compiler that </span></p></li><li><p><span>//  only z.x or z.y will be accessed in any given scope. union z { </span></p></li></ul><p><br /></p><p><span>int * __restrict x; </span></p><p><span>double * __restrict y; }; </span></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span>Code </span></p><p><span>MD-UML </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>int * __restrict x; </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>226 </span></p><p><span>Copyright © 1998-2015 No Magic, Inc. </span></p><p><span>C++ CODE ENGINEERING </span><span>Microsoft C++ Profiles </span></p><p><span>__forceinline, __inline </span></p><p><span>The insertion (called inline expansion or inlining) occurs only if the compiler's cost/benefit analysis show it to be profitable. Inline expansion alleviates the function-call overhead at the potential cost of larger code size. </span></p><p><span>The </span><span>__forceinline </span><span>keyword overrides the cost/benefit analysis and relies on the judgment of the programmer instead. Exercise caution when using </span><span>__forceinline</span><span>. Indiscriminate use of </span><span>__forceinline </span><span>can result in larger code with only marginal performance gains or, in some cases, even performance losses (due to increased pag- ing of a larger executable, for example). </span></p><p><span>Using inline functions can make your program faster because they eliminate the overhead associated with function calls. Functions expanded inline are subject to code optimizations not available to normal functions. </span></p><p><span>The compiler treats the inline expansion options and keywords as suggestions. There is no guarantee that functions will be inlined. You cannot force the compiler to inline a particular function, even with the </span><span>__forcein- line </span><span>keyword. When compiling with </span><span>/clr</span><span>, the compiler will not inline a function if there are security attributes applied to the function. </span></p><p><span>The </span><span>inline </span><span>keyword is available only in C++. The </span><span>__inline </span><span>and </span><span>__forceinline </span><span>keywords are available in both C and C++. For compatibility with previous versions, </span><span>_inline </span><span>is a synonym for </span><span>__inline</span><span>. </span></p><p><br /></p><p><span>Grammar: </span></p><p><span>__inline </span><span>function_declarator</span><span>; </span><span>__forceinline </span><span>function_declarator</span><span>; </span></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>__interface IEvents { </pre><pre>  };</pre><pre>class CSource { </pre><pre>public:</pre><pre>   __event __interface IEvents; </pre><pre>};</pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span><br /></span></p><p><span>Code </span></p><p><span>__inline int max( int a , int b ) { if( a &gt; b ) </span></p><pre><span> return a; return b; </span></pre><p><span>} </span></p><p><span>MD-UML</span></p><h3><span>C++ Attributes </span></h3><p><span>Attributes are designed to simplify COM programming and .NET Framework common language runtime development. When you include attributes in your source files, the compiler works with provider DLLs to insert code or modify the code in the generated object files. </span></p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Code</th><th>MD-UML</th></tr><tr><td><pre>[ coclass,</pre><pre>aggregatable(allowed), </pre><pre>  uuid(&quot;1a8369cc-1c91-</pre><pre>42c4-befa-5a5d8c9d2529&quot;)] </pre><pre>class CMyClass {};</pre><pre><br /></pre></td><td><p>__event __interface IEvents;</p><p>is mapped to attribute without name </p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673550 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00678: Known Limitations and Constraints

- page_id: `169673550`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673550/Known+Limitations+and+Constraints
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-01-15T09:40:00.258+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Importing data from other UML tools and formats > Enterprise Architect Import
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following tableshows the conversion limitations and constraints in EAexported XMIand EAImport.

| No. | Constraint name | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | N-ary Association | Due to the difference between the EA exported XMI and MagicDraw, CEA or CSM XMI, the view of will not be imported. |
| 2 | Diagram Legend | MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM does not have a similar element. Diagram legends will not be imported. |
| 3 | Diagram Note | The element most similar to Diagram Note is the MagicDraw, CEA and CSM diagram information table (Option > Show diagram info). However, in EA it will be mapped to the Option > Show diagram details. So Diagram Notes will not be imported to MagicDraw, CEA or CSM. |
| 4 | Text size | The size of text displayed in EA and MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM may vary. The text size in EA is mostly larger. |
| 5 | Word wrap | Due to size constraints in text displayed in element blocks, the result of word wrap in EA and MagicDraw, CEA, and CSM is likely to be different. |
| 6 | Contact point between element and link | The position of both ends of a link element cannot be mapped to MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM because the last segments of the link element in MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM always point to the center of connected elements. |
| 7 | Display alternative image | In EA, you can display an element in a diagram by using an alternative image. MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM will not import that particular image and will use a default shape instead. |
| 8 | Link label position | A Link element, such as an Association, has many labels, including multiplicity and role name labels. MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM will not import the position of these labels and will use a default position instead. |
| 9 | Pin position | A position in MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM may have been slightly moved from its original position in EA. If the pin is placed at the corner of its containing element, it will be shifted a little away from the corner (mostly in a clockwise direction). |
| 10 | Nested CallBehavior- Action | A element nested with another CallBehaviorAction element. The outermost part of the element will remain. The other will be removed. |
| 11 | DataStoreNode inner element | A DataStoreNode element containing Activity Diagram related elements. Every element inside that particular DataStoreNode element will be removed. Only the DataStoreNode element will remain. |
| 12 | Object inner element | An Object element containing Activity Diagram related elements. Every element inside that particular Object element will be removed. Only the Object element will remain. |
| 13 | Lifeline position | The position in MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM will not correspond to the original position in EA. The position is fixed. |
| 14 | Diagonal Sequence Message | MagicDraw, CEA, and CSM do not support Diagonal Sequence Messages. |
| 15 | Sequence Activation Options | MagicDraw, CEA, and CSM do not support manipulating Sequence Activation through Sequence Activation Options. |
| 16 | State contains diagram | A diagram element placed inside a element will be removed. |
| 17 | Region in Orthogonal State | EA exported XMI contains incorrect information when:more than one region has identical names.a region was created and then removed before exporting to XMI.This will cause an unexpected result after importing it into MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM. |
| 18 | Assembly Relationship in Composite Structure diagram | Assembly relationships in EA are exported to XMI as Connectors. They will then be imported to MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM as . |
| 19 | Interaction elements as diagram frame | MagicDraw, CEA, and CSM do not import Interaction elements displayed as a diagram frame in an diagram. |
| 20 | Message timing details | Duration Observation, and Timing Observation are not imported to MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM. |
| 21 | Concurrent State Regions in StateMachine | Adding and removing multiple Concurrent State Regions to and from StateMachine in EA can cause the EA XMI to be incorrectly exported. If the EA exported XMI is in this state, the result of the imported to MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM cannot be determined.The same problem also occurs if there are multiple Concurrent State Regions with the same name. |
| 22 | Combined Fragment | Adding and removing multiple Interaction Operands to and from Combined Fragment in EA can cause the EA XMI to be incorrectly exported. The problem can be fixed by importing the EA XMI back to a new project in EA and exporting it back before importing it to MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM. |
| 23 | Problem occurs during conversion of . | Importing EA XMI to MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM sometimes results in the following error message: “Problem occurred during conversion of Sequence diagram”. If this occurs, please try to import the XMI back to a new project in EA and export it back again before importing it to MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><span>The following table </span><span>shows the conversion limitations and constraints in EA<span> </span>exported XMI<span> </span>and EA<span> </span>Import.</span></p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>No.</th><th>Constraint name</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td>1</td><td><span style="color: black;">N-ary<span> </span>Association</span></td><td><span style="color: black;">Due to<span> </span>the di<span>f</span>ference between the EA<span> </span>exported XMI<span> </span>and MagicDraw, CEA or CSM XMI, </span><span style="color: black;">the view of<span> </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Association Class" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[N-ary association]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> will not be imported.</span></td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td><span style="color: black;">Diagram Legend</span></td><td><p><span style="color: black;">Ma</span><span style="color: black;">gicDraw, CEA, or CSM does not have a similar element. Diagram legends will not be </span><span style="color: black;">imported.</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>3</td><td><span style="color: black;">Diagram Note</span></td><td><p>The element most similar to Diagram Note is the MagicDraw, CEA and CSM diagram information table (<strong>Option </strong>&gt; <strong>Show diagram info</strong>). However, in EA it will be mapped to the <strong>Option </strong>&gt; <strong>Show diagram details</strong>. So Diagram Notes will not be imported to MagicDraw, CEA or CSM.</p></td></tr><tr><td>4</td><td>Text size</td><td><p>The size of text displayed in EA and MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM may vary. The text size in EA is mostly larger.</p></td></tr><tr><td>5</td><td><p>Word wrap</p></td><td><span style="color: black;">Due to<span> </span>size constraints in text<span> </span>displayed in element blocks, the result of </span><span style="color: black;">word wrap in EA<span> </span>and MagicDraw, CEA, and CSM is likely to<span> </span>be di<span>f</span>ferent.</span></td></tr><tr><td>6</td><td><span style="color: black;">Contact point </span><span style="color: black;">between<span> </span>element<span> </span>and link</span></td><td><span>The position of<span> </span>both ends of<span> </span>a link element cannot be mapped to<span> </span>MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM<span> </span>because<span> </span>the<span> </span>last<span> </span>segments<span> </span>of<span> </span>the<span> </span>link<span> </span>element<span> </span>in<span> </span>MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM</span><span> </span>always point to<span> </span>the center of<span> </span>connected elements.</span></td></tr><tr><td>7</td><td><span style="color: black;">Display alternative </span><span style="color: black;">image</span></td><td><p>In EA, you can display an element in a diagram by using an alternative image. MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM</span> will not import that particular image and will use a default shape instead.</p></td></tr><tr><td>8</td><td><span style="color: black;">Link<span> </span>label<span> </span>position       <span> </span></span></td><td><span style="color: black;">A<span> </span>Link<span> </span>element,<span> </span>such<span> </span>as<span> </span>an<span> </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Association" /><ac:link-body>Association,<span> </span></ac:link-body></ac:link>has<span> </span>many<span> </span>labels,<span> including</span> </span><span style="color: black;">multiplicity<span> </span>and<span> </span>role<span> </span>name<span> </span>labels.<span> </span>MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM</span><span> </span>will<span> </span>not<span> </span>import<span> </span>the<span> </span>position<span> </span>of these labels and will use a<span> </span>default position instead.</span></td></tr><tr><td>9</td><td><span style="color: black;">Pin position</span></td><td><p>A <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Pin" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Pin ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>position in MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM</span> may have been slightly moved from its original position in EA. If the pin is placed at the corner of its containing element, it will be shifted a little away from the corner (mostly in a clockwise direction).</p></td></tr><tr><td>10</td><td><span style="color: black;">Nested CallBehavior- </span><span style="color: black;">Action</span></td><td><p>A <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Call Behavior Action" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[CallBehaviorAction ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>element nested with another CallBehaviorAction element. The outermost part of the element will remain. The other will be removed.</p></td></tr><tr><td>11</td><td><span style="color: black;">DataStoreNode inner </span><span style="color: black;">element</span></td><td><p>A DataStoreNode element containing Activity Diagram related elements. Every element inside that particular DataStoreNode element will be removed. Only the DataStoreNode element will remain.</p></td></tr><tr><td>12</td><td><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Object inner element</span></td><td><span style="color: black;">An<span> </span>Object<span> </span>element<span> </span>containing<span> </span>Activity Diagram related elements.<span> </span>Every </span><span style="color: black;">element inside that<span> </span>particular Object element will be removed. Only the Object element will remain.</span></td></tr><tr><td>13</td><td><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Lifeline position</span></td><td><p>The <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Lifeline" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Lifeline ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>position in MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM</span> will not correspond to the original position in EA. The position is fixed.</p></td></tr><tr><td>14</td><td><span>Diagonal Sequence Message</span></td><td><p>MagicDraw<span>, CEA, and CSM</span> do not support Diagonal Sequence Messages.</p></td></tr><tr><td>15</td><td><p>Sequence Activation Options</p></td><td><p>MagicDraw<span>, CEA, and CSM</span> do not support manipulating Sequence Activation through Sequence Activation Options.</p></td></tr><tr><td>16</td><td><span style="color: black;">State<span> </span>contains dia</span><span style="color: black;">gram</span></td><td><p>A diagram element placed inside a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="State" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[State ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>element will be removed.</p></td></tr><tr><td>17</td><td><span style="color: black;">Region in Orthogo</span><span style="color: black;">nal State</span></td><td><p>EA exported XMI contains incorrect information when:</p><ul><li>more than one region has identical names.</li><li>a region was created and then removed before exporting to XMI.</li></ul><p>This will cause an unexpected result after importing it into MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM</span>.</p></td></tr><tr><td>18</td><td><span style="color: black;">Assembly Relation</span><span style="color: black;">ship in Composite Structure diagram</span></td><td><p>Assembly relationships in EA are exported to XMI as Connectors. They will then be imported to MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM</span> as <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Connector" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Connectors]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></td></tr><tr><td>19</td><td><span style="color: black;">Interaction elements </span><span style="color: black;">as diagram frame</span></td><td><p>MagicDraw<span>, CEA, and CSM</span> do not import Interaction elements displayed as a diagram frame in an <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Interaction Overview diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Interaction Overview]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> diagram.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">20</td><td colspan="1"><span style="color: black;">Message timing </span><span style="color: black;">details</span></td><td colspan="1"><p>Duration Observation, and Timing Observation are not imported to MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM</span>.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">21</td><td colspan="1"><span style="color: black;">Concurrent State </span><span style="color: black;">Regions in StateMachine</span></td><td colspan="1"><p>Adding and removing multiple Concurrent State Regions to and from StateMachine in EA can cause the EA XMI to be incorrectly exported. If the EA exported XMI is in this state, the result of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="State" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[StateMachine]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> imported to MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM </span>cannot be determined.</p><p>The same problem also occurs if there are multiple Concurrent State Regions with the same name.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">22</td><td colspan="1"><span style="color: black;">Combined Fragment</span></td><td colspan="1"><p>Adding and removing multiple Interaction Operands to and from Combined Fragment in EA can cause the EA XMI to be incorrectly exported. The problem can be fixed by importing the EA XMI back to a new project in EA and exporting it back before importing it to MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM</span>.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">23</td><td colspan="1"><span style="color: black;">Problem<span> </span>occurs<span> </span>during </span><span style="color: black;">conversion of <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /></ac:link>.</span></td><td colspan="1"><p>Importing EA XMI to MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM </span>sometimes results in the following error message: “Problem occurred during conversion of Sequence diagram”. If this occurs, please try to import the XMI back to a new project in EA and export it back again before importing it to MagicDraw<span>, CEA, or CSM</span>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169674795 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00679: Label layout in the diagram

- page_id: `169674795`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169674795/Label+layout+in+the+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2022-10-21T11:14:20.180+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Layout
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Default label positions

Default label positions are retained after moving a path, shape, or related element if it is a semantically logical decision. See the example below, where association multiplicities remain at their default positions after class is moved.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

If labels are at their default position, reset labels position functionality is not available.

##### Label positions after moving a path, shape, or related element

After moving a path, shape, or related element, default label positions are retained if it is a semantically logical decision.

For a nicer representation of labels in a diagram, the label positions are reset to default automatically if:

1. Symbol properties are edited. When symbol properties are edited by adding or removing a label text box in the diagram pane, label positions are reset.
2. Path, path end, or port properties are edited. When path, path end, or port data are edited by adding or removing the label text box in the diagram pane, label positions are reset.
3. Path, shape, or related element are moved.

To check the label deviation from its default position

- Select a label and begin to move it. The dashed line shows the deviation from the default position. This helps to show the current label's owner.

When the label is not in its default position, you may indicate it by the icon on the bottom right corner, which appears after selecting the label's owner.

##### Label consideration mode

When arranging shapes for the hierarchic layout mechanisms, you may choose the **Label Consideration** mode:

- Hierarchic Layout.
- Class Diagram Layout.
- Composite Diagram Layout.
- Activity Diagram Layout.
- State Machine Diagram Layout.
- Business Process Diagram Layout.
- Ordered Hierarchic Layout.

In order to do this, you must specify the **Label consideration** mode (Full, Partial, or None) in the **Diagram Layout Options** dialog.

To specify the label consideration mode

1. From the diagram toolbar, expand [ATTACHMENT filename='quick_diagram_layout.png'] and select Layout Options command.
2. On the left side of the dialog, select one of the following layout styles: - Under the General Layout group, select Hierarchic Layout . - Under the Specialized Layout group, select any layout style:
  - Class Diagram Layout.
  - Composite Diagram Layout.
  - Activity Diagram Layout.
  - State Machine Diagram Layout.
  - Business Process Diagram Layout.
  - Ordered Hierarchic Layout.
3. On the right side of the dialog, find the Label consideration option.
4. Click on the value of this option and select one of the following modes:
  - None - does not consider any labels, does not increase the shape size.
  - Partial - considers only the center path labels, does not increase shape sizes by labels.
  - Full - considers all labels, creates additional space for them and increases the shape sizes.
5. Click the Layout button.
6. Click OK .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

does not consider any labels, does not increase the shape size.</li><li><strong>Partial</strong> - considers only the center path labels, does not increase shape sizes by labels.</li><li><strong>Full</strong> - considers all labels, creates additional space for them and increases the shape sizes.</li></ul></li><li>Click the <strong>Layout</strong> button.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169674797 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00680: Label orientation

- page_id: `169674797`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169674797/Label+orientation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2022-10-21T11:26:38.021+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Layout
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Label Orientation symbol property

1409097048

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Label Orientation symbol property

1409097051

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Label Orientation symbol property

1409097050

You can optimize diagram layout by changing the orientation of symbol labels. Rotate labels horizontally or vertically, or select the automatic label orientation option, which adjusts labels according to the position of shapes or paths.

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### This sample diagrams illustrate how changing label orientation can optimize diagram layout.

To change the orientation of independent labels

1. In an open diagram, select one or several labels that you want to rotate.
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the diagram toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='rotate_label.png'] to change the label orientation from horizontal to vertical or vice versa.
  - In the diagram toolbar, click the arrow next to [ATTACHMENT filename='rotate_label.png'] and from the menu select the desired label orientation option: Horizontal , Vertical , or Automatic .

The labels are rotated to the selected position. If you select the **Automatic** label orientation option, labels are rotated according to the position of shapes or paths they are on.

To change the orientation of labels on a shape or path

1. In an open diagram, select the path or shape on which you want to rotate labels.
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the diagram toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='rotate_label.png'] to change the label orientation from horizontal to vertical or vice versa.
  - In the diagram toolbar, click the arrow next to [ATTACHMENT filename='rotate_label.png'] and from the menu select the desired label orientation option: Horizontal , Vertical , or Automatic .

The labels on the shape or path are rotated to the selected position. If you select the **Automatic** label orientation option, labels are rotated according to the position of the shape or path.

#### NOTE: Label Orientation symbol property

Label Orientation symbol property

When you change the orientation of individual labels or labels displayed on a shape or path, the value of the **Label Orientation** symbol property of the related shapes or paths changes as well. To open the **Symbol Properties** dialog, right-click a symbol in a diagram and select **Symbol Properties**.

##### Default label orientation

By default, all labels on shapes and paths are displayed horizontally. You can change the default label orientation for a project as explained below.

To change the default label orientation

1. In the main menu of a modeling tool, select Options > Project .
2. On the left side of the Project Options dialog, select Symbol styles > Default > Shapes project option group.
3. On the right side of the dialog, click the property value box of the Label Orientation property and select one of the following values:
  - Horizontal - to display the labels of newly created shapes horizontally.
  - Vertical - to display the labels of newly created shapes vertically.
  - Automatic - to display the labels of newly created shapes according to their position in a diagram.
4. On the left side of the Project Options dialog, select Symbol styles > Default > Paths project option group.
5. On the right side of the dialog, click the property value box of the Label Orientation property and select one of the following values:
  - Horizontal - to display the labels of newly created paths horizontally.
  - Vertical - to display the labels of newly created paths vertically.
  - Automatic - to display the labels of newly created paths according to their position in a diagram.
6. Click OK on the bottom of the Project Options dialog.

The labels of newly created symbols in the diagrams of a project will be displayed according to your selected property values.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

## PAGE 00681: Language Properties

- page_id: `169679417`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679417/Language+Properties

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169673399 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00682: Launchers

- page_id: `169673399`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673399/Launchers
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2022-04-22T09:15:50.239+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Getting started
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

221276213

INLINE

221276216

INLINE

221276215

INLINE

Launchers show which programs will be used for opening different file types in the application. Add new, edit, or remove launchers and specify their properties (file extension, program path or name, program arguments).

To open the **Launcher Properties** dialog

1. From the **Options** menu, choose **Environment**.
2. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Environment Options' space='MD2024xR1'] dialog, from the options group list select **Launchers**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To specify properties of the default launchers

1. To edit the Default launcher, click the**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**button next to the **Default Launcher** or**Default Browser** text boxes.
2. The Launcher Properties dialog appears.
3. You can change Launcher`s executable file and arguments.

To create the defined launcher

1. Click the Add button, found below the Defined Launchers list.
2. The Launcher Properties dialog appears.
3. Specify the file extension, which will be opened with the specified launcher.
4. Type the name of the launcher in the text box, or browse to the location of the launcher by clickingthe [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button.
5. Specify external launcher arguments.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Properties are described in the following table.

| Property Name | Function |
| --- | --- |
| Extensions | Type the extensions of the files which will be opened with the specified launcher. You can define more than one extension. Separate them using commas (i.e., html, xls, doc, cpp, h). |
| Launcher | An executable file with which the extension file will be opened. |
| Arguments | Arguments are required by the external launcher, so the modeling tool provides the ability to pass some common arguments to the launcher:$f - a file name$md_dir - the modeling tool installation directory$prj_dir - the project directory (if the project is saved)$r - a row (used when opening Code Engineering object source code file with "Edit Source" action)$c - a column (used when opening Code Engineering object source code file with "Edit Source" action). |

221276212

INLINE

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing environment options' space='MD2024xR1']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169674793 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00683: Layout

- page_id: `169674793`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169674793/Layout
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T15:04:53.943+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

903811718

903811721

903811720

The diagram layout engine is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.

It is easy to manage simple or complex diagrams using automated layout features that optimize diagram layout.

Symbols are automatically laid out on diagrams when displaying related elements, creating diagrams by using wizards, and creating related diagrams.

You can layout diagrams quickly by using the Quick Diagram layout. The quick layout is a recommended type of layout, and it is specific for each diagram.

The layout mechanism is built on various layout tools. There are 2 groups of layout tools: general layout tools and specific diagram layout tools. These are the general layout tools:

- Orthogonal Layout
- Hierarchic Layout
- Organic Layout
- Tree Layout
- Circular Layout
- Oblique Path Router
- Rectilinear Path Router

These layout tools are provided by yFiles layout tool component. You can arrange each diagram (except the Sequence diagram) by using any of the following general layout tools:

- Class Diagram Layout
- Composite Structure Diagram Layout
- Activity Diagram Layout
- State Machine Diagram Layout
- Business Process Diagram Layout

To layout diagrams by using the quick diagram layout, do one of the following

- Press Ctrl+Q.
- On the diagram toolbar, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='quick_diagram_layout.gif'] Quick Diagram Layout button.
- On the Main menu, click Layout > Quick Diagram Layout .

To layout diagrams by using layout tools, do one of the following

- On the Main menu, click Layout and select one of the layout tools.
- On the diagram toolbar, click the small arrow next to the [ATTACHMENT filename='quick_diagram_layout.gif'] Quick Diagram Layout button, and select one of the layout tools.
- Select the element symbols you want to layout, right-click any of them, and in the shortcut menu, select Layout , then select one of the layout options.

To change the diagram layout options

1. On the Main menu, click Layout > Layout Options .
2. In the Diagram Layout Options dialog, specify the layout options.
3. Click OK .

903811717

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagramming' space='MD2024xR1']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169674832 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00684: Layout template creation mode

- page_id: `169674832`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169674832/Layout+template+creation+mode
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-11-07T08:46:08.528+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Layout templates > Creating layout template
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Before creating layout templates from Composite Structure Diagrams, you can select whether layout templates are created as Class Diagrams (default option) or Composite Structure Diagrams. To do this, specify layout template creation mode as described below.

To specify layout template creation mode

1. In the main menu of your modeling tool, select Options > Project .
2. On the left side of the Project Options dialog, expand the General node and select the General option group.
3. Click the specification cell of the Layout Template Creation Mode option on the left side of the dialog and select one of the following values as shown below:
  - Usage - if you want to create layout templates as Composite Structure Diagrams.
  - Definition - if you want to create layout templates as Class Diagrams.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Specifying layout template creation mode.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Before creating layout templates from Composite Structure Diagrams, you can select whether layout templates are created as Class Diagrams (default option) or Composite Structure Diagrams. To do this, specify layout template creation mode as described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To specify layout template creation mode</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu of your modeling tool, select <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project</strong>.</li><li>On the left side of the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog, expand the <strong>General</strong> node and select the <strong>General</strong> option group.</li><li>Click the specification cell of the <strong>Layout Template Creation Mode</strong> option on the left side of the dialog and select one of the following values as shown below:<br /><ul><li><strong>Usage</strong> - if you want to create layout templates as Composite Structure Diagrams.</li><li><strong>Definition</strong> - if you want to create layout templates as Class Diagrams.</li></ul></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="layout_template_creation_mode.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Layout template creation mode" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Specifying layout template creation mode.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169674807 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00685: Layout templates

- page_id: `169674807`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169674807/Layout+templates
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T11:59:55.074+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Important

1350756844

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Important

1350756847

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Important

1350756846

#### EXCERPT: Important

INLINE

The layout templates functionality, which is driven by <<layoutTemplate>> stereotype, assists you in creating well-formed diagrams. It allows you to define Class appearance once, and reuse it later when creating Parts typed by that Class and Ports in different diagrams. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part' space='MD2024xR1'] and their Ports are represented identically as part's type [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MD2024xR1'] whose layout and style is defined in the layout template diagram. Often, Port position reflects its physical location in the system, so it is beneficial to maintain the same appearance of Parts, layout, and style of Ports throughout the whole model. In the figure below, the appearance of the *Modem Card* Block is the same in two different diagrams, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR1'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='SYSMLP2024xR1'].

700500

#### INFO: Additional information

Additional information

You can reuse layout templates in other projects by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting packages to new projects' space='MD2024xR1']

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Usage of a layout template. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.

You can create a layout template in the Class Diagram or derive it from an existing Composite Structure Diagram as described below.

To define a layout template in the Class Diagram and apply it to other diagrams:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining layout template' space='MD2024xR1']
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting diagram as layout template' space='MD2024xR1']
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying layout template' space='MD2024xR1']

To derive a layout template for the Composite Structure Diagram and apply it to other diagrams:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Layout template creation mode' space='MD2024xR1']
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating layout template' space='MD2024xR1']
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying layout template' space='MD2024xR1']

#### WARNING: Important

Important

Only one Class shape with its Ports can be defined in one layout template diagram.

Read the following pages to learn how to manage layout templates:

- Stop using Class diagram as a layout template
- Removing applied layout template from shape
- Multiple layout template
- Setting layout template as default
- Navigation to layout templates
- Using layout templates with diagram aspects

1350756842

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Composite Structure diagram' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagram aspects' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting packages to new projects' space='MD2024xR1']

**Sample model**

The sample model used in the figure of this page is **Modem Cable** which you can download *[ATTACHMENT filename='Modem Cable Layout Templates.mdzip'].*

It allows you to define Class appearance once, and reuse it later when creating Parts typed by that Class and Ports in different diagrams. <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Part" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Parts]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and their Ports are represented identically as part's type <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class" /></ac:link> whose layout and style is defined in the layout template diagram. Often, Port position reflects its physical location in the system, so it is beneficial to maintain the same appearance of Parts, layout, and style of Ports throughout the whole model. In the figure below, the appearance of the <em>Modem Card</em> Block is the same in two different diagrams, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR1" ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML Block Definition diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR1" ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML Internal Block diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="eb0321fa-0304-49f9-a789-864cfc06e6a9"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">700</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UtnKfP58ET0&amp;feature=youtu.be" /></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="height">500</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7be13953-1f28-42da-ae50-f4aeb153dd51"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Additional information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can reuse layout templates in other projects by <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting packages to new projects" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[exporting packages to new projects.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="block_layout_templates.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Layout templates" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Usage of a layout template. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.</h6><p>You can create a layout template in the Class Diagram or derive it from an existing Composite Structure Diagram as described below.</p><p><br />To define a layout template in the Class Diagram and apply it to other diagrams:</p><ol><li><p><span class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Defining layout template" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Define the appearance of Class, layout, and style of its Ports in the Class diagram.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></p></li><li><p><span class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Setting diagram as layout template" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Set that Class diagram as the layout template.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></p></li><li><p><span class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Applying layout template" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Apply the layout template to other diagrams.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></p></li></ol><p class="confluence-link">To derive a layout template for the Composite Structure Diagram and apply it to other diagrams:</p><ol><li class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Layout template creation mode" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specify whether layout templates are created as Class Diagrams or Composite Structure Diagrams.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Creating layout template" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create a layout template from a Part Property in the Composite Structure Diagram.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Applying layout template" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Apply the layout template to other diagrams.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ol><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e8579265-ff01-4631-a7bb-2ec607d5bbd0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Important</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Only one Class shape with its Ports can be defined in one layout template diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Read the following pages to learn how to manage layout templates:</p><ul class="list-children not-expandable"><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Stop+using+Class+diagram+as+a+layout+template">Stop using Class diagram as a layout template</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Removing+applied+layout+template+from+shape">Removing applied layout template from shape</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Multiple+layout+template">Multiple layout template</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Setting+layout+template+as+default">Setting layout template as default</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Navigation+to+layout+templates">Navigation to layout templates</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Using+layout+templates+with+diagram+aspects">Using layout templates with diagram aspects</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b163ff8f-d870-4097-8963-e1241d22b243"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1350756842</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Composite Structure diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Diagram aspects" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting packages to new projects" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Sample model</strong></p><p>The sample model used in the figure of this page is <strong>Modem Cable</strong> which you can download <span class="confluence-link"><em> <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="Modem Cable Layout Templates.mdzip"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Sample models" /></ri:attachment></ac:link>.</em></span></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
